CONDITIONS OF ADMISSION AND AUTHORIZATION FOR TREATMENT
This consent applies to all Norton Healthcare physician practice and Immediate Care Center locations.
CONSENT TO DIAGNOSTIC TESTS, PROCEDURES, AND MEDICAL TREATMENT:
I voluntarily consent to care that involves routine diagnostic tests, procedures, and medical treatment as prescribed by my physician and performed by the
facility. No guarantee has been given by anyone as to the results that may be obtained This consent includes testing for Hepatitis, Human
Immunodeficiency Virus (HIV) or any other blood-borne infectious diseases, if ordered for diagnostic purposes or due to an occupational exposure. I
consent to photographing, videotaping, video monitoring and audio devices for medical, scientific, safety or education purposes as well as for the purposes
of identification. I understand that these will be stored in a secure manner that will protect my privacy. Images that identify me will only be released
when authorized by me or as needed to provide for my continued medical treatment or identification.
OTHER PRACTITIONERS AND SUPPLIERS OF SERVICES:
I understand and acknowledge that I may require the services of physicians, groups of physicians or other practitioners (such as nurses and physician
assistants) who are not employees of the facility, including, but not limited to, anesthesiologists, radiologists, emergency department physicians, certified
registered nurse anesthetists, and other practitioners. I also understand that residents, fellows, medical students and students of various health care fields
may be involved in my care and that they are not employees of the facility. I agree that the signing of this document does not in any way imply that
Norton Healthcare is responsible for or assumes any liability for the activities of any of such physicians or practitioners who are not its employees.
When a physician, other practitioner or supplier of services bills me directly, I agree to pay the bill. I agree that the provisions regarding the release of
information and assignment of benefits set out in paragraphs III, V, and VI below shall apply not only to the facility but also to such physicians,
practitioners and suppliers of services and their claims for payment.
RELEASE OF INFORMATION:
I authorize the facility to notify my referring and treating physician(s) of my admission to the facility and to release any information about me as
requested by such physician(s). I authorize the facility and any other holder of medical or other information about me, including medical information
concerning drug related conditions, alcoholism, psychological conditions, psychiatric conditions, and/or blood-borne infectious diseases, to release to the
Social Security Administration, its intermediaries and its carriers, State agencies, the review organization employed by my employer, or the employer of
the insured member of my family, or any private third-party payor, as appropriate, any information required for the completion of any claim for benefits
arising out of services rendered to me either on an inpatient or outpatient basis. I further authorize the release of my social security number to the
manufacturer of any implanted medical device in accordance with the Medical Device Tracking Act of the Food and Drug Administration (FDA). I
authorize the release of my HIV test results to health care personnel in the event of an occupational exposure. If my injury is work-related, I authorize
the facility and any other holder of medical or other information about me to release from my medical record any information as requested by the
company and/or its workers compensation administrator as indicated below:

ASSIGNMENT OF BENEFITS:

RELEASE OF INSURANCE INFORMATION TO FACILITY:
I agree to the release by my insurance carrier to the facility and the facility’s billing service of any eligibility and utilization data concerning my insurance
coverage which the facility and/or its billing supplier may require. I authorize the facility to file an appeal of a payment denial and/or request a hearing
regarding any denial of payment for treatment provided to me. I further authorize the facility to present proof, utilize facility records and my records
and documentation for appeals and hearings.
VII.
VI.
PAYMENT OF FACILITY BILLS WHEN DUE:
I am liable for the payment of the account of the facility and I am obligated to pay the account of the facility, in accordance with the regular rates and
terms of the facility. The benefits and financial responsibility are calculated based on In-Network Benefits. Benefits and eligibility are subject to change
and are not a guarantee of payment. If my injury is work-related, I am further obligated to pay the account of the facility in the event my employer or its
third-party administrator denies payment for workers compensation. It is understood that the uninsured portion of bills are payable at the end of each
week and in full upon discharge from the facility. I understand that after reasonable notice, delinquent accounts may be turned over to a collection agency
and/or an attorney for collection. Should it be necessary to place this account in the hands of an attorney for collection, I agree to pay the costs of
collection, including any court costs and a reasonable fee to the attorney employed by the facility to collect the account. I agree that the facility, its
employees, agents, attorneys, and collection agencies, may call or text the cell number I provide for any reason, including but not limited to inquiring
about the status and collection of my account. I also expressly authorize that each of them may use any mechanism, including but not limited to
automatic dialing, artificial and pre-recorded voice systems.

Dear Dr. Fannin NMA,
I would like to give you an update after our visit today. In an effort to keep unnecessary paperwork from
being generated, I am sending a short synopsis of my note. I would be happy to forward you a complete
note upon request.
ASSESSMENT/S:
eustachian tube dysfunction, headache, Chronic sinusitis, unspecified location, Postnasal drip, Perennial
allergic rhinitis, and lymphadenopathy
SUMMARY:
1-28-16 This is a very pleasant 39 year old who reports a long history of allergic rhinitis ever since
childhood. He has had adenotonsillectomy as well as at least 3 sets of tubes as a child also with no
significant ear infections as an adult. He does report intermittent paranasal headaches with frequent
nasal congestion and postnasal drainage and associated cough. He also has had intermittent swelling in
the region of his left parotid at times. On exam he has tympanosclerosis bilaterally without middle ear
effusion or inflammation, he has a mild septal deviation of left with allergic nasal findings and no acute
inflammation anteriorly or posteriorly on endoscopy, he has no suspicious lymphadenopathy and no
signs of parotid inflammation currently. He has had vision side effects with Flonase in the past so I
suggested a trial of Astelin as well as daily Zyrtec sinus rinses. He does have interest in allergy workup
and I can see him after this is completed.
I appreciate the opportunity to be involved with the care of this patient. Please feel free to contact me
with any questions - cell [PHONE-34].
Wes A. Allison, M.D.

SUMMARY:
[DOB-7186] This is a very pleasant 40 year old who returns reporting worsening allergies over the past
year despite trials of Flonase and Zyrtec. He reports nasal trauma 2 months ago with a possible nasal
injury. On exam his nose is midline with no evidence of recent trauma, he continues to have a stable
septal deviation to the left with allergic nasal findings posteriorly and no acute inflammation on
endoscopy. He is agreeable allergy workup and I can see him after this is completed. We’ll continue a
daily spray and antihistamine regimen otherwise.
I appreciate the opportunity to be involved with the care of this patient. Please feel free to contact me
with any questions - cell [PHONE-46].

Dear Dr. Fannin NMA,
I would like to give you an update after our visit today. In an effort to keep unnecessary paperwork from
being generated, I am sending a short synopsis of my note. I would be happy to forward you a complete
note upon request.
ASSESSMENT/S:
eustachian tube dysfunction, headache, Chronic sinusitis, unspecified location, Postnasal drip, Perennial
allergic rhinitis, and lymphadenopathy
SUMMARY:
4/14/17-This is a very pleasant 40 year old who returns after allergy workup was performed and we
discussed these results in detail. His testing showed severe allergies across-the-board. He had mild
obstructive findings on spirometry. On exam, he has tympanosclerosis bilaterally without middle ear
effusion or inflammation, he has a stable septal deviation to the left without acute sinonasal
inflammation. We discussed the risks and benefits of immunotherapy and he will give this consideration
as he may be switching insurances soon. I suggested he start Claritin and Zyrtec in the meantime as our
test results can be used for up to one year should he wish to pursue immunotherapy. I can see him
again in six months to follow his progress or sooner for worsening symptoms. Of note, he was unable to
use Flonase due to side effects.
I appreciate the opportunity to be involved with the care of this patient. Please feel free to contact me
with any questions - cell [PHONE-7178].
CBH

Dear Dr. Fannin NMA,
I would liketo give you an update after our visit today.In an effort to keep unnecessary paperwork from
being generated, I am sending a short synopsis of my note. I would be happy to forward you a complete
note upon request.
ASSESSMENT/S:
eustachian tube dysfunction, headache, Chronic sinusitis, unspecified location, Postnasal drip, Perennial
allergic rhinitis, and lymphadenopathy
SUMMARY:
6-14-19:This is a very pleasant 43 year old who recently underwent allergy testing and these results
were discussed in detail today.Testing showed moderate to severe allergies nearly across the board
most Significantly to dust, dogs, a mold, a weed and white ash trees. Of note he reports improvement in
his itching and erythema after a medrol pack was prescribed after testing 2 days ago as he reported a
scratchy throat and itching all over similar to hayfever. He added Singulair to his daily regimen of Claritin
prior to testing and reports resolution of his allergic rhinitis symptoms at that time. His exam is
unchanged today. We discussed the risks and benefits of starting escalation immunotherapy and we
both agree on holding off on treatment at this time and continuing conservative management. He will use
a daily regimen to include sinus rinses, Flonase Astelin, Claritin and Singulair and we will plan on seeing
him back in 6 months or sooner if needed.
I appreciate the opportunity to be involved with the care of this patient. Please feel free to contact me
with ahy questions - cell[PHONE-7170].

History of Present Illness
Patient is a 47yo male that is presenting with a history of a
complex chronic anal fistulas bilaterally. The patient has had one
LIFT procedure in the past that has failed. The patient has had
prior fistulotomy on the left side and seton placement on the right
side. In July it was appreciated that the patient had a posterior
midline fistula. The patient has his seton fall out of the right side
and underwent subcutaneous fistulotomy and seton placement.
The patient is still suffering from fistula drainage and is presenting
today for fistulotomy and possible seton placement.

PIT Note
Patient: KIPER. JAMES R
Age: 46 years Sex: Male DOB: 4/29/1976
Associated Diagnoses: None
Author: BELL, STEPHEN,PA-EMR
MRN: V000416364 FIN:V[PHONE-147]
HPI:
46-year-old male presents to triage as a restrained ever involved in a 2 vehicle collision earlier today. Patient was at a low
stop sign when he was rear-ended by another vehicle going about 5 mph he got out of his vehicle and went to get some
information and insurance, and then the guy stuck around for about 3 or 4 minutes and they took off in his vehicle than 2
blocks later that person got into the other accident that occurred 1 more time as well. He complains of neck and low back
pain. No airbag deployment. No compartment intrusion. Did not hit his head on anything in the vehicle. No loss
consciousness. No vision changes or vomiting. Complains of mild headache as well. Rates his pain a 4 out of 10.
History review:
Past medical, surgical and social history reviewed in the nursing notes. I agree with these notes.

Basic Information

12/5/2022 20:40 EST Chief Complaint Chief Complaint .
History of Present Illness
The patient presents following motor vehicle collision. The onset was 7 hours ago. The Collision was rear impact and low speed. The patient was
the driver. There were safety mechanisms including seat belt, lower back, neck, felt like he went forward and hit the seat abruptly . The degree of pain
is minimal. The degree of bleeding is none. Risk factors consist of none. Therapy today: none. Associated symptoms: back pain, denies chest pain,
denies loss of consciousness, denies altered level of consciousness,denies dizziness and denies syncope. Additional history: happened 4 hr prior to
arrival, ambulatory on scene.

Skin symptoms: No rash,

Endocrine symptoms: No polyuria,
Hematologic/Lymphatic symptoms: Bleeding tendency negative, bruising tendency negative.
Allergy/immunologic symptoms: No food allergies.

KAVALUKAS, SANDRA, MD-PRO ([DOB-7037] 10:24 EST)
Performed by:KAVALUKAS, SANDRA, MD-PRO on December 22, 2021 10:24 EST
CC: here to discuss second stage surgery for anal fistula
History of Present Illness:
Post op seton placement for anal fistula 11/4. Doing well, minimal drainage. Stopped taking fiber as he feels that
constipated him, taking stool softener instead. No fever/chills. Stopped abx after 10 day course ending 3 days after
surgery. Has great difficulty evacuating - states often has to try various positions,strains at times,feels like he has had
pelvic floor dysfunction type symptoms for a while. Also complains of anal sensation/fullness which was present prior to
seton insertion and continues after.

• Anal fistula

History And Physical-Consultation
Left lower leg: No edema.

General: Skin is warm and dry.
Capillary Refill:Capillary refill takes less than 2 seconds.

Mental Status: He is alert and oriented to person, place, and time.

Anorectal Exam:
Inspection: Normal
Anal Fissure: None
Perianal Skin: Fistula
Perianal Skin: no pilonidal sinus
External Hemorrhoid: no
Sphincter Tone: Tight
Seton in place, transsphincteric and still appears to have significant amount of muscle/dives deep. Unable to get
good exam given tight sphincter and tenderness on DRE.
Visit Diagnoses:
1. Anal fistula
Assessment. Management and Plan:
45 yo male with transsphincteric anal fistula, state post scion placement 11/4.
Discussed in depth definitive repair of this, with illustrations.
Would l ike to do LIFT if possible, as both LIFT and ERAF have 65-70% success rate, and if recurs, presumably LIFT
should recur as intersphincteric allowing for sphincterotomy.
However, if his tract is as deep as I think it is, or perhaps even seems to go suprasphincteric, then will need to perform
ERAF. Both of these carry a small but present risk of incontinence - with his anal spasm and tone I think this is highly
unlikely.
Also discussed defecatory dysfunction - 1 think he will benefit from Pelvic Floor PT and he agrees. Discussed will work
on this postop after definitive repair of fistula.
Sandra Lee Kavalukas, MD
UOFL PHYSICIANS - COLON & RECTAL SURGERY

• Incision and Drainage Rectal Abscess ([DOB-7011])

Mr. Kiper is a 46 yo gentleman with a complex recurrent anal
fistula which has failed a LIFT procedure. He is here today for a
second attempt at definitive repair.
Review of Systems
Constitutional: No fevers, chills, sweats
HEENT: No ear pain, nasal congestion, sore throat
Respiratory: No shortness of breath, cough, pain on breathing,
sputum production
Cardiovascular: No Chest pain, No palpitations
Gastrointestinal: No nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, constipation
Musculoskeletal: No muscle or extremity pain
lntegumentary:No rash, pruritus, abrasions, lesions
Neurologic: No weakness, numbness
Physical Exam
General: Lying in bed, no acute distress
Eyes: Pupils equal and reactive to light, 4mm bilaterally
HENT: Normocephalic, moist oral mucosa
Neck: Trachea midiine
Heart:Normal rate, regular rhythm
Chest: No crepitus or obvious deformity
Respiratory: Equal chest rise, unlabored breathing on room air
Gastrointestinal:Soft, nontender, nondistended,no bruising
Neuro: No focal deficits
Skin: No lesions or lacerations
MSK: No muscle tenderness or limb deformity
Assessment and Plan
46 yo M with complex anal fistula
- OR today for EUA with Hanley procedure, LIFT vs ERAF

Patient Name: Kiper,James

Norton Healthcare Telemedicine Informed Consent
I understand that telemedicine is the use of electronic information and communication technologies by a health care provider to
deliver services to an individual when he/she is located at a different site than the provider; and hereby consent to Norton
Healthcare providing health care services to me via telemedicine. The information discussed may be used for diagnosis, therapy,
follow-up and/or education.
Expected Benefits:
•Improved access to care by enabling a patient to obtain services from providers at distant sites.
•Patient remains closer to home where local healthcare providers can maintain continuity of care.
•Reduced need to travel for the patient or other provider.
The Process:
You will be introduced to the provider, such as residents, fellows, medical students and/or students of various health care fields who
are not employees of Norton Healthcare. You may ask questions of the provider or any telemedicine staff in the room with you, if you
are unsure of what is happening. If you are not comfortable with seeing a provider on videoconferencing technology, you may reject
the use of the technology and schedule a traditional face-to-face encounter at any time. Safety measures have been implemented to
ensure that this videoconference is secure, and no part of the encounter will be recorded without your written consent.
Possible Risks:
There are potential risks associated with the use of telemedicine which include, but may not be limited to:
•A provider may determine that the telehealth encounter is not yielding sufficient information to make an appropriate clinical
decision.
•Technology problems may delay medical evaluation and treatment for your encounter.
•In very rare instances, security protocols could fail, causing a breach of privacy of personal medical information.
By Signing this Form, I understand the following:
1.1 understand that the laws that protect privacy and confidentiality of medical information also apply to telemedicine, and that no
information obtained in the use of telemedicine which identifies me will be disclosed to researchers or other entities without my
consent.
2.1understand that I have the right to withdraw my consent to the use of telemedicine in the course of my care at any time, without
affecting my right to future care or treatment.
3.1also understand that if the provider believes I would be better served by a traditional face-to-face encounter, they may, at any
time stop the telemedicine visit and schedule a face-to-face visit.
4. 1 understand that I may expect the anticipated benefits from the use of telemedicine in my care, but that no results can be
guaranteed or assured.
Patient Consent to the Use of Telemedicine:
I have read and understand the information provided above regarding telemedicine, and all of my questions have been answered to
my satisfaction. T hereby give my informed consent for the use of telemedicine in my care.
I hereby authorize Norton Healthcare to use telemedicine in the course of my diagnosis and treatment.

KAVALUKAS,SANDRA,MD-PRO (11/4/2021 17:44 EDT)
Operative Report
PATIENT DOB:4/29/l976 DATE OF SERVICE: [DOB-6942]
REFERRING PHYSICIAN: TERRENCE P DONOHUE, MD
SURGEON: Sandra Kavalukas
PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:
Anal fistula.
POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:
Transsphincteric anal fistula.
ASSISTANTS:
There are no assistants.
ANESTHESIA:
LMA.
POSITIONING:
Candy-cane lithotomy.
SPECIMENS:
None.
PRINCIPAL PROCEDURES:
1. Anoscopy.
2. Placement of transsphincteric anal seton. Ins and outs are per anesthetic
care record.
INDICATIONS FOR OPERATION:
Mr. Kiper is a 45-year-old male, who has been complaining of recurrent anal
abscesses on the right side with pain and drainage. An external opening was
visualized in the office, highly suspicious for an anal fistula.
DESCRIPTION OF PROCEDURE:
After informed consent was obtained, the patient was taken back to the
operating room, placed supine on the operating room table. LMA anesthesia was
induced. He was then placed in candy-cane lithotomy and the perineum was
Report Request ID: 141495080 Page 1 of 3 11/4/2021 18:08 EDT
J.KIPER-M-000082

Male
Operative-Procedure Reports
propped and draped in normal sterile fashion. I began by injecting 20 mL of
0.25% Marcaine, 1% lidocaine with epinephrine mixture into the ischiorectal
fat in the intersphincteric space for a local block. I then performed a
digital rectal exam low and posterior on the right side, there was an area of
rough regularity in the anal canal. I have been visualized this by inserting
a small and then a medium and then a large Sawyer retractor, to where I could
see some heaped up granulation tissue distal to the dentate line inside the
anal canal. 1 then injected hydrogen peroxide into the external opening,
which was approximately 5 cm away from the anal canal in the right posterior
direction. Hydrogen peroxide did come out inside the anal canal, confirming
presence of a fistulous tract.
1 then took a fistula probe and very gently guided it through fistula tract to
the area, where my finger could feel the probe coming through the area, where
the defect was internally. Of note, this tract went quite deep through the
ischiorectal fat before coming through the sphincters. 1 placed a silk tie
through the fistula probe, brought it out through the external opening and
then tied this to a vessel loop, which I then used in a rondezvous backward
type fashion to pull it back through the anal fistula and used a hemostat to
keep the ends of the vessel loop together. At this point, I then tried to
make the fistula tract as short as possible. I extended the external opening
by making the incision with the Bovie approximately 1.5 cm closer to the anal
canal and curetted out the abscess cavity under the external opening
to help allow this to heal. 1 irrigated both the anal canal into the abscess
cavity with saline and assured hemostasis. At this point,I then tied the
seton together with a series of 3-0 silk ties in a teardrop configuration.
So, this would not completely turn inside the anal canal cause him pain and
blockage. At this point, finally before ending the procedure, 1 placed an
anal retractor back into the anal canal and thoroughly visualized exactly how
he is going to prepare for my second-stage. Given the depth of this fistula,
I feel that a lift would be quite difficult and he does have some anal mucosa
to perform an endorectal advancement flap, which was likely what he will need
at the second-stage once the inflammation goes down.
1 finished the procedure by injecting another 20 mL of local anesthesia into
the area around, where I did both a curette and the external opening
extension. 1 placed bacitracin over these wounds. 1 also packed the cavity
around the external opening with a small amount of iodoform Nu Gauze. We then
placed clean sterile dressing. The patient was taken to recovery room in
satisfactory condition.
Sandra Kavalukas

KAVALUKAS, SANDRA, MD-PRO ([DOB-6918] 14:50 EST)
Operative Summary
PATIENT DOB:4/29/1976 DATE OF SURGERY: [DOB-314]
PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS(ES): Transsphincteric anal fistula.
POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS(ES):
Transsphincteric anal fistula.
PROCEDURE PERFORMED: Exam under anesthesia, ligation of intersphincteric
fistula tract.
SURGEON: Sandra Kavalukas
ASSISTANT: Jonathon Vacek, MD, Resident.
ESTIMATED BLOOD LOSS: 10 mL.
ANESTHESIA: General endotracheal.
POSITION: Prone.
SPECIMENS: None.
INDICATIONS FOR OPERATION: This is a 45-year-old male who previously had a
seton placed for a somewhat deep tracking anal fistula that went through the
external and internal anal sphincters toward the tract went deep in the
intersphincteric groove, however, then sharply curved back toward the lowlying internal opening. He had several weeks for the inflammation to resolve
and he is here for definitive repair.
PROCEDURE IN DETAIL: After informed consent was obtained, the patient was
taken back to the operating room. General endotracheal anesthesia was induced
on the stretcher. He was then placed in prone jack-knife position. His
perineum was prepped and draped in normal sterile fashion. A time-out was
performed. We then did a digital rectal exam where we can see some nodularity
and feel an internal opening just on the left posterolateral aspect of the
anal canal. He also had some granulation tissue from his previous fistulotomy
with an attempt to make that track shorter. I curetted this out to make that
wound base clean again. We then took a fistula probe and passed it through
Report Request ID: 144917157 Page 1 of 2 [DOB-6916] 09:42 EST
J.KIPER-M-000088

NORTON HEALTHCARE
200 E Chestnut St
LOUISVILLE KY 40202

Operative-Procedure Reports
the area where the seton was,again this was somewhat deep. However, I did
feel that I may be able to palpate and get down to this fistula tract, so we
started our intersphincteric incision. This was done in a linear fashion in
the intersphincteric groove, used the Lone Star retractor to help retract the
skin on the way, and then we gained access to the intersphincteric plane
without taking any external sphincter muscle. We bluntly dissected down with
a hemostat gently spreading until I was completely surrounded the fistula
tract. I grabbed this with a Kelly clamp and attempted to move both the seton
and the probe and we did indeed have the tract above us. At this point, we
passed two 2-0 Vicryl ties, tried to elongate the tract as much possible, cut
down with a 15 blade until we actually saw the metal for the fistula probe and
then backed the probe out. At this point,I took 2-0 Vicryl on UR-6 and tied
a figure-of-eight suture on both the internal and external side of this
fistula tract and then continued to divide it. We then went back behind that
using that as a handle and through several more throws of the 2-0 Vicryl
suture to completely close the fistula tract. We then tested this with
hydrogen peroxide from both the external opening side where there was no
leakage and then the internal opening side, we did still have some leakage, so
we continued to throw some more throws of the stitch until this was completely
tied down.
We then irrigated out this dissection plane, assured hemostasis, and the last
thing that we did before we started to dose was to appose the ends of the
fistula tract. Therefore, I took a stitch through the superior aspect of the
internal opening area and tied this to the inferior aspect of the external
opening areas that we could offset these fistula tracts to be laid them from
just growing back together. We then closed the deep dermal stitch with a 3-0
Vicryl, closed the skin with 3-0 chromic, and left the curetted fistula area
out to be tacked. At this point, the patient was taken to the recovery room
in satisfactory condition.
SANDRA KAVALUKAS

Recurrent anal fistula.

There are no assistants.
PROCEDURES PERFORMED:
1 . Exam under anesthesia.
2. Incision and drainage of wound with small scroma.
3. Placement of a transsphincteric seton.

There are no specimens.

General endotracheal.

Prone.
PROCEDURE IN DETAIL:
After informed consent was obtained, the patient was taken back to the
operating room and placed under general endotracheal anesthesia, and then
flipped into the prone jack-knife position. Perineum was propped and draped
in normal sterile fashion. I performed a time-out. I injected 20 mL of 0.25%
Marcaine and 1% lidocaine with epinephrine near the external aspect of the
incision. He had a completely healed medial aspect of his incision. However,
the lateral 0.5 cm had heaped up friable skin with some minor drainage. I began by taking 15-

blade to open this up. I did not get any underlying purulence. I did get a
small amount of serosanguineous drainage from this area. I went through the
subcutaneous tissues with a hemostat, gaining access again into somewhat of
the external opening cavity with the suction. At this point,
I placed a fistula probe and through this, 1 could easily see this
coming out inside the rectum in an area of a very large internal opening that
was in the setting of a posterior midline fissure. This was made basically to
left of the midline, however, was in the area of a very large mucosal defect,
I then rendezvoued a silk stitch in a vessel loop through this area to place a
seton, which I tied down with the silks, continued to irrigate out along the
external opening, and then finished the procedure. I injected more local
anesthesia. The patient was taken to the recovery room in satisfactory
condition.

REFERRING PHYSICIAN: TERRENCE P DONOHUE, MD
SURGEON: Sandra Kavalukas
PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:
Right-sided anal abscess.
POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS:
Right-sided anal abscess.
PRINCIPAL PROCEDURE:
Incision and drainage of right-sided ischiorectal abscess with placement of
subcutaneous seton.
ASSISTANT:
Sarah Couch, MD, Resident
ESTIMATED BLOOD LOSS:
5 cc.
ANESTHESIA:
LMA.
POSITIONING:
Candy-cane lithotomy.
SPECIMENS:
None.
INDICATIONS FOR OPERATION:
Mr. Kiper is a 45-year-old male, who previously had a left-sided anal fistula.
I attempted a lift repair of this, which then failed and he developed an
abscess on the left side. I looked back again and replaced the seton into
this anal fistula tract. He then subsequently began complaining of some mild
right-sided pain on the ischiorectal area, which had been progressively
worsening over past month. I ordered a CT scan, which did not show any fluid
Report Request ID: 152086893 Page 1 of 2 3/31/2022 10:07 EDT

Operative-Procedure Reports
in the horseshoe deep post anal space, though he did have an obvious abscess
on the right side.
PROCEDURE IN DETAIL:
After informed consent was obtained, the patient was taken back to the
operating room and placed supine on the operating room table. LMA anesthesia
was induced. He was placed in candy-cane lithotomy. Perineum was prepped and
draped in normal sterile fashion and a time-out was performed. Injected 20 cc
of 0.25% Marcaine and 1% lidocaine with epinephrine as a block in the area
that we were planning on performing the maximal amount of incision. Just
prior to doing this,I performed digital rectal exam. The anal sphincter was
tight. However, overall, the mucosa felt healthy and most importantly his
posterior midline anal fissure appeared to be markedly better healed. I
inserted a small and then very gingerly a medium Sawyer retractor and indeed
his anal fissure had healed quite a bit in the left side, where the seton was
and the left-sided external opening was very quiescent and appeared to be
healing well. We therefore turned our attention to incision and drainage of
the right-sided perianal abscess.
The Bovie electrocautery made a 2 cm incision in the point of maximum
fluctuance. immediately, he got out some purulent drainage,opened this
cavity up with the hemostats and irrigated this out. I did inject hydrogen
peroxide into the cavity to see if this would communicate over to the left
side and come out the area of the seton, however, it did not. I then next
placed the fistula probe into the cavity which did track posteriorly toward
the coccyx, however, did not cross the midline or find any fistulous
connections or communications over to the left side. Because I was worried
about a blind and reforming abscess after the skin healed,I then placed a
seton subcutaneously through the deepest part of the tracking, but used this
only as a vessel loop to serve as a Penrose drain. We then irrigated out
again, assured hemostasis and packed the cavity with iodoform packing. The
patient was taken to recovery room in satisfactory condition.
Sandra Kavalukas

Operative Summary
PATIENT DOB:4/29/1976 DATE OF SURGERY: [DOB-6892]
PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS(ES): Transsphincteric anal fistula with horseshoe
component.
POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS(ES): Transsphincteric anal fistula with horseshoe
component.
PROCEDURE PERFORMED: Modified Hanley procedure with intersphincteric seton
placement.
SURGEON: Sandra Kavalukas
ATTENDING PHYSICIAN: Sandra Kavalukas.
ASSISTANTS:
1.Susan Galandiuk, MD.
2.Dylan Thomas Carroll, MD, Resident.
SPECIMENS: None.
IN'S AND OUT'S: Per anesthetic care record.
INDICATIONS FOR OPERATION: Mr. Kiper is a 46-year-old male had a
posterolateral anorectal fistula in the base of the fissure. The opening was
in the posterior midline and a seton was placed. I then went to perform a
LIFT procedure on this, which failed and he also developed an abscess on the
right side with a few air bubbles going into the deep post anal space, though
no clear fistula was identified due to scar tissue. At that point,I placed
another seton in the right subcutaneous space for the abscess and he is now
here for definitive repair.
PROCEDURE IN DETAIL: After informed consent was obtained,the patient was
taken back to the operating room, placed supine on the operative room table.
A Foley catheter was placed. He was placed in Allen stirrups. His perineum
was prepped and draped in normal sterile fashion. Time-out was performed. We
began by taking a Shepherd hook fistula probe, placing it into the posterior
midline defect, and then palpating out to the intersphincteric groove and

Operative-Procedure Reports
dissecting down to this by making a short incision through the anal skin,
dissecting in the intersphincteric space, and passing a silk tie through this
fistula probe and then vessel loop backwards through to create a seton in the
posterior midline space. We then tried to connect the external opening of the
left-sided fistula tract to the external opening of this midline fistula.
This was quite difficult due to large amount of scar tissue. However, we were
able to finally pass this through and we placed a seton outside of the anal
canal from this left external opening to the midline external opening and
removed our seton that was going into the anal canal from this left-sided
tract. We then took the seton out of the right side, debrided out that wound,
and did not find any communication or fistulous tract to the midline.
We then curetted out the fistula tracts, assured hemostasis, placed local
anesthesia. The patient was returned to the recovery room in satisfactory
condition.

KAVALUKAS,SANDRA,MD-PRO (7/9/2023 11:03 EDT)
Operative Summary
PATIENT DOB:4/29/1976 DATE OF SURGERY: [DOB-6884]
PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS(ES): History of horseshoe abscess with
transsphincteric anal fistula.
POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS(ES): History of horseshoe abscess with
transsphincteric anal fistula.
PROCEDURE PERFORMED:
1. Removal of posterior midline intersphincteric anal fistula with seton with
primary fistulotomy of the posterior internal anal sphincter.
2. Subcutaneous fistulotomy of right posterior anal fistula with placement of
cutting seton around the external sphincter on the right side.
3. Exploration of left external opening.
SURGEON: Sandra Kavalukas
PROCEDURE IN DETAIL: After informed consent was obtained,the patient was
taken back to the operating room and placed supine on the operating room
table. General endotracheal anesthesia was induced. He was placed in
candy-cane lithotomy. His perineum was prepped and draped in normal sterile
fashion. Time-out was performed. We began by injecting 20 mL of 0.25%
Marcaine and 1% lidocaine with epinephrine in the ischiorectal fat and
intersphincteric space for perianal block. Because the patient has a history
of significant posterior midline anal fissure.I only went up to a
medium-sized Sawyer retractor. Iwas able to see the seton that was with the
internal opening approximately 4 mm into the anal canal and the external
opening was in the intersphincteric space, this was created at our last
operation and we performed a primary fistulotomy and curetted out the edges of
this fistula tract. While I had the Sawyer retractor in the anal canal, I
then injected hydrogen peroxide into both openings. He had an opening in the
right posterior aspect and to the left posterior aspect. The right posterior
aspect did tract to the area where this internal opening was from our previous
fistulotomy. The left posterior midline, despite having put approximately 30
mL to 40 mL of hydrogen peroxide into this tract, never came out into the anal
canal. Therefore, we simply made the external opening on the left side
slightly larger, curetted it out and packed this lightly with packing. The
right posterior anal fistula, we were easily able to get a fistula probe

through this area. It appeared to come out in the intersphincteric plane of
where this is open. Once I cut open the skin and exposed the muscle,I cut
through some of the subcutaneous fat as well as shortened the tract and then
the muscle that remained,Itook 0 Ethibond, looped this around the muscle for
planned cutting external sphincter cutting seton, which will be tightened
later. We curetted out the tract, marsupialized the edges with a 4-0
Monocry!, and washed out the entire area and irrigated this thoroughly. We
then injected local anesthesia. The patient was taken to recovery room in

Operative Summary
PATIENT DOB:4/29/1976 DATE OF SURGERY: [DOB-6874]
PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS(ES): Anal fistula.
POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS(ES): Anal fistula.
PROCEDURE PERFORMED: Completion fistulotomy of cutting seton and placement of
left posterior anal fistula seton.
SURGEON: Sandra Kavalukas
ASSISTANT: Dylan Thomas Carroll, MD, Resident.
ESTIMATED BLOOD LOSS: 2 mL.
ANESTHESIA: General endotracheal.
POSITIONING: Lithotomy.
SPECIMENS: None.
IN'S AND OUT'S: Per anesthetic care record.
PROCEDURE IN DETAIL: After informed consent was obtained,the patient was
taken back to the operating room and placed supine on the operating room
table. General endotracheal anesthesia was induced and placed in candy-cane
lithotomy. His perineum was propped and draped in a normal sterile fashion.
Time-out was performed. We injected 20 mL of 0.25% Marcaine and 1% lidocaine
with epinephrine in the ischiorectal fat in the intersphincteric space for
perianal block. We then inserted a small and then a medium Sawyer retractor.
The inside of the anal canal, his fissure had healed, however,the area, where
his cutting seton was, we placed a fistula probe through this. This was
luckily about 2 mm of muscle fibers. We did a completion sphincterotomy and
removed the seton. We then lightly curetted out the tract and bovied this to
induce granulation tissue, in the left posterior aspect, approximately 4 cm
away from the anal verge, another heaped up area. Once we injected hydrogen
peroxide into this, we immediately had hydrogen peroxide inside the anal
canal, signs that the fistula had recurred. We were able to replace the seton

KAVALUKAS,SANDRA,MD-PRO (2/19/2024 10:27 EST)
Operative Summary
PATIENT DOB:4/29/1976 DATE OF SURGERY: [DOB-6866]
PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS(ES): Recurring transsphincteric anal fistula.
POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS(ES): Recurring transsphincteric anal fistula.
PROCEDURE PERFORMED: Ligation of intersphincteric fistula tract.
SURGEON: Sandra Kavalukas
ASSISTANT: Alan Sumski, MD.
ESTIMATED BLOOD LOSS: 5 mL,
ANESTHESIA: General endotracheal.
POSITION: Prone.
SPECIMENS: None.
IN'S AND OUT'S: Per anesthetic care record.
PROCEDURE IN DETAIL: After informed consent was obtained, the patient was
taken back to the operating room, placed under general endotracheal anesthesia
on the stretcher,then flipped to prone jack-knife position. His perineum was
prepped and draped in normal sterile fashion. Time-out was performed. We
placed a Lone Star retractor to get the anal canal in stretch. Then placed a
20 mL of 0.25% Marcaine and 1% lidocaine with epinephrine in the ischiorectal
fat In the intersphincteric space for perianal block. Ithen placed a fistula
probe through the fistula tract. This was slightly difficult given the amount
of scarring that he had around his anal canal. However, I then removed the
seton after the fistula probe was through the tract and I made an
intersphincteric incision along the groove with the Bovie electrocautery
extending approximately 3 cm. We then very carefully divided the internal
from the external sphincter going down the intersphincteric space and
skeletonizing the fistula tract. I dissected around it on either side with a
hemostat and finally came around the deep portion of It with a right angle
clamp. I then took two 2-0 Vicryl ties, passed these through the right angle
Report Request ID: 202755648 Page 1 of 2 2/20/2024 04:05 EST
J.KIPER-M-000103

Operative-Procedure Reports
and then once we pulled out the fistula probe, we tied these far apart from
each other as possible and divided the fistula tract in the middle with a #15
blade. We then threw several more figure-of-eights of 2-0 Vicryl sutures on
either side of the internal opening of the fistula tract and tested both sides
with hydrogen peroxide on Angiocath and continued for any repairing stitches
until there was no more leakage of hydrogen peroxide into this defect. I then
threw several figure-of-eight sutures over the internal opening. We irrigated
out the wound and then closed the incision. After we apposed the fistula
tracts with another Vicryl suture, so that they would not be right next to
each other. We then closed the defect with deep dermal 2-0 Vicryls and then
ran the skin with 3-0 Monocryl. We placed more local anesthesia in the wound
and placed bacitracin over the incision. I curetted out the external opening
and Bovied this to full granulation tissue. The patient was then taken to
recovery in satisfactory condition.

Operative Summary
PATIENT DOB:4/29/1976 DATE OF SURGERY: [DOB-384]
PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS(ES): Possible anal fistula.
POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS(ES): Possible anal fistula.
PROCEDURE PERFORMED: Exam under anesthesia with plaeement of subcutaneous
Ethibond seton and transsphincteric Ethibond seton.
SURGEON: Sandra Kavalukas
ASSISTANT: Mohammed I Ranavaya, MD.
SECONDARY SURGEON: Jeffrey Jorden, MD
ESTIMATED BLOOD LOSS: None.
ANESTHESIA: General endotracheal.
POSITIONING: C’andy-cane lithotomy.
IN'S AND OUT'S: Per anesthetic care record.
SPECIMENS: None.
PROCEDURE IN DETAIL: After informed consent was obtained, the patient was
taken back to the operating room and placed supine on the operating room
table. General endotracheal anesthesia was induced. He was placed in
candy-cane lithotomy. His perineum was prepped and draped in normal sterile
fashion. Time-out was performed. We began by injecting 20 mL of 0.25%
Marcaine and 1% lidocaine with epinephrine in the ischiorectal fat in the
intersphincteric space for perianal block. He had healed skin and some minor
drainage from the left posterolateral aspect where the seton was. 1 was able
to open this in the skin edge and injected hydrogen peroxide with the anal
retractor in the anal canal and did not see hydrogen peroxide. We carefully
look a fistula probe. We tracked further posteriorly and somewhat towards the
midline into an empty cavity and this was consistent with his complaints of
symptomatic pressure with occasional drainage. My thought at that point was

Operative-Procedure Reports
lo simply place a subcutaneous seton and see if this would continue to allow
this lo heal, as we did not want this abscess cavity to continue draining. At this point,since the patient had
so many previous surgeries, I had Dr. Jeffrey Jorden come in for consultation.
He also injected a fair bit of hydrogen peroxide into the external opening
and never got anything on the internal anal canal side. He also took the
fistula probe and was able to relatively easily lace it through the posterior
midline tract and this actually did come out in the anal canal. Since that
opening had been recreated or just coaxed open, we placed a #1 Ethibond seton
through this tract that went transsphineterically into the anal canal and I
continued to make a small counter incision to make a subcutaneous seton so
that, hopefully, in about a month, I can pull out the transsphincteric one and
leave the external subcutaneous one only to sec if this will heal up. At that
point, we injected more local anesthesia. The patient was taken to recovery
room in satisfactory condition.

Exam: Cervical Spine MRI
DATE OF EXAM: [DOB-6826]
Referring Physician: Donohue,
Terrence M.D.
CLINICAL HISTORY: 40 year-old male with neck pain and bilateral upper extremity radiculopathy since
MVA 7/12/15. Evaluate for cervical spine abnormality.
TECHNIQUE: Imaging is performed on a 1.5 Tesla system. Multiple weightings including T1 and T2 were
acquired in multiple imaging planes.
FINDINGS: The C1-T2 vertebrae are visualized. The vertebral bodies are normal in height and signal intensity.
The cervical spinal cord is normal in morphology and signal intensity. Imaged portions of the cerebellum, pons,
and brainstem are unremarkable.
C2-3: Unremarkable.
C3-4: There is a minimal central disc bulge. The spinal canal and neural foramina are patent.
C4-5, C5-6: Unremarkable.
C6-7: There is 1-2 mm left paracentral disc bulging. The spinal canal and neural foramina are patent.
C7-T1, T1-T2: Unremarkable.
The surrounding soft tissues, as imaged are unremarkable.
IMPRESSION:
1. At C3-4, there is minimal central disc bulging, and at C6-7. There is 1-2 mm left paracentral disc bulging.
Spinal canal and neural foramina are patent.
2. No other bulge or herniation.

General Information
Patient is a 35 year old male c/o pain and mobility Issues in bilateral shoulders. The patient had come in before for shoulder and neck pain,
but was not compliant with FT or exercises and decided to stop. He went back to the MD and had imaging done which found bilateral scapular winging due to periscapular weakness.He had an MRI of hisc-spine then saw a Neurosurgeon who told him it wasn'tsurgical and that he had some mild bulging discs and they weren'
t enough to be causing the pain In the shoulders/arms.
No new PMH significant.

General Information
Padent Is a 35 year oid male c/o pain and mobility Issues in bilateral shoulders.The patient had come In before for shoulder and neck pain,
but was not compliant with PT or exercises and decided to stop. He went back to the MD and had imaging done which found bilateral
scapular winging due to perlscapular weakness. He had an MRI of his c-spine then saw a Neurosurgeon who told him it wasn't surgical and
that he had some mild bulging discs and they weren't enough to be causing the pain in the shouiders/arms.
Pain in left shoulder
Other specified acquired deformities of musculoskeletal system
Pain in right shoulder

Assessment
The patient tolerated today’s treatment intervention without complaints of pain or difficulty. The patient was educated regarding their
diagnosis,prognosis,related pathology & plan of care. The patient demonstrates a good understanding of the risks,benefits,
precautions/contraindications,&. prognosis of their skilled rehabilitation program.
Presentation:
•James has met 3 of 7 goals. He is making good progress with PT towards unmet goals. His posture appears to be much improved but he
needs to still work on scapular strength and shoulder mobility.All unmet goals extended today and HEP reviewed with patient.
Recommendations: Skilled Intervention: Required To:
•Improve Motor Control. Increase Strength. Increase Range of Motion.

Left Shoulder
The patient was instructed in the independent performance of a home exercise program that addresses the problems and achieving the
goals outlined in the plan of care.
Amount,Frequency and Duration:
•Frequency and Duration:It is recommended that the patient attend rehabilitative therapy for 2 visits a week with an expected duration of
2 weeks. The outlined therapeutic procedures and services in the plan of care will address the problems and goals identified.
Therapeutic Contents:
•Active Assistive Range of Motion Activities. Active Range of Motion Activities. Client Education. Home Exercise Program. Joint
Mobilization Techniques, Manual Range of Motion Activities. Manual Therapy Techniques. Neuromuscular Re-education. Passive Range
of Motion Activities, Proprioceptive/Closed Kinetic Chain Activities. Self Care/Home Management. Soft Tissue Mobilization Techniques,
Stretching/Flexibility Activities. Therapeutic Activities. Therapeutic Exercise. Trigger Point Dry Needle.
•Modalities:
•Cryotherapy. Electrical Stimulation (E-Stim):Unattended. Interferential Stimulation. Moist Hot Pack. Ultrasound.

Assessment
The patient tolerated today's treatment Intervention without complaints of pain or difficulty. The patient was educated regarding their
diagnosis,prognosis,related pathology & plan of care. The patient demonstrates a good understanding of the risks, benefits,
precautions/contraindications,& prognosis of their skilled rehabilitation program.
Presentation:
•James has met 3 of 7 goals. He is making good progress with FT towards unmet goals, His posture appears to be much improved but he
needs to still work on scapular strength and shoulder mobility. All unmet goals extended today and HEP reviewed with patient.
Recommendations: Skilled Intervention: Required To:
•Improve Motor Control. Increase Strength. Increase Range of Motion.

[DOB-6727] - Office Visit in Norton Community Medical Associates - Audubon West 200 (continued)
Clinical Notes (continued)
Subjective:
Patient ID: James R Kiper is a 38 yr/o male.
Hand Pain
The incident occurred more than 1 week ago. The incident occurred at home. The injury mechanism was a fall. The 
pain is present in the left forearm, left hand, right forearm and right hand. The quality of the pain is described as aching 
and shooting. The pain radiates to the left arm. The pain is moderate. The pain has been fluctuating since the incident. 
Associated symptoms include numbness and tingling. Pertinent negatives include no chest pain or muscle weakness. 
The symptoms are aggravated by lifting, palpation and movement. He has tried NSAIDs and immobilization for the 
symptoms. The treatment provided mild relief. 
Hypothyroidism
This is a chronic problem. The current episode started more than 1 year ago. The problem occurs constantly. The 
problem has been unchanged. Associated symptoms include arthralgias, joint swelling and numbness. Pertinent 
negatives include no abdominal pain, anorexia, change in bowel habit, chest pain, chills, congestion, coughing, 
diaphoresis, fatigue, fever, headaches, myalgias, nausea, neck pain, rash, sore throat, swollen glands, urinary 
symptoms, vertigo, visual change, vomiting or weakness. Nothing aggravates the symptoms. Treatments tried: 
synthroid. The treatment provided significant relief. 
Rash
This is a recurrent problem. The current episode started more than 1 year ago. The problem has been waxing and 
waning since onset. The affected locations include the abdomen and right lower leg. The rash is characterized by 
itchiness and redness. It is unknown if there was an exposure to a precipitant. Pertinent negatives include no anorexia, 
congestion, cough, diarrhea, eye pain, facial edema, fatigue, fever, joint pain, nail changes, rhinorrhea, shortness of 
breath, sore throat or vomiting. Past treatments include nothing. His past medical history is significant for allergies. 
There is no history of asthma or eczema. 
The following portions of the patient's history were reviewed and updated as appropriate: allergies, current 
medications, past family history, past medical history, past social history, past surgical history and problem list.
Review of Systems 
Constitutional: Negative. Negative for fever, chills, diaphoresis, activity change, appetite change, fatigue and 
unexpected weight change. 
HENT: Negative for congestion, sore throat, rhinorrhea, trouble swallowing, neck pain and voice change. 
Eyes: Negative. Negative for pain. 
Respiratory: Negative. Negative for cough and shortness of breath. 
Cardiovascular: Negative. Negative for chest pain. 
Gastrointestinal: Positive for constipation. Negative for nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, diarrhea, anorexia and 
change in bowel habit. 
 History of hemorrhoids and anal fissure
Genitourinary: Negative. 
Musculoskeletal: Positive for joint swelling and arthralgias. Negative for myalgias and joint pain. 
Skin: Negative for nail changes and rash. 
Neurological: Positive for tingling and numbness. Negative for vertigo, weakness and headaches. 
Hematological: Negative. 
Psychiatric/Behavioral: Negative. 
All other systems reviewed and are negative.

Patient Summary as-of Visit (continued)
Clinical Notes
Subjective:
Patient ID: James R Kiper is a 38 yr/o male.
Back Pain
This is a recurrent problem. The problem has been waxing and waning since onset. The pain is present in the lumbar 
spine. The quality of the pain is described as aching, cramping and shooting. The pain is moderate. Pertinent 
negatives include no abdominal pain, bladder incontinence, bowel incontinence, chest pain, dysuria, fever, headaches, 
leg pain, numbness, paresis, paresthesias, pelvic pain, perianal numbness, tingling, weakness or weight loss. 
Sinusitis
This is a new problem. The current episode started 1 to 4 weeks ago. The problem is unchanged. The pain is mild. 
Associated symptoms include congestion, coughing, sinus pressure, sneezing, a sore throat and swollen glands. 
Pertinent negatives include no headaches. Past treatments include oral decongestants. The treatment provided no 
relief. 
The following portions of the patient's history were reviewed and updated as appropriate: allergies, current 
medications, past family history, past medical history, past social history, past surgical history and problem list.
Review of Systems 
Constitutional: Negative for fever and weight loss. 
HENT: Positive for congestion, sore throat, sneezing and sinus pressure. Respiratory: Positive for cough. Cardiovascular: Negative for chest pain. 
Gastrointestinal: Negative for abdominal pain and bowel incontinence. 
 Itchy rash in rectal area
History of anal fissures - yet to follow up with surgery

Patient Summary as-of Visit (continued)
Clinical Notes
Subjective:
Patient ID: James R Kiper is a 38 yr/o male.
HPI Comments: Here for evaluation of recurrent lymph node swelling and perianal irritation. 
States that he has developed recurrent left sided non tender lymph node swelling that comes and goes for over 3-4 
months. Not associated with any symptoms that he has noted. No associated with infection, colds, URI's or other 
illness. Notes a "throbbing" sensation when present. Swelling lasts a 2-3 days and resolves on its own. Occasionally 
he feels he notes some erythema over the swollen area. No itching, burning, or pain. 
Also with chronic persistent perianal irritation. Has seen derm who prescribed antifungal topical cream which he states 
has provided no relief. Has tried steroid cream and barrier creams with no relief. States he has a flat erythematous 
lesion that comes and goes and occasionally bleeds. pmhx significant for anal fissures and hemorrhoids. 
The following portions of the patient's history were reviewed and updated as appropriate: allergies, current 
medications, past family history, past medical history, past social history, past surgical history and problem list.
Review of Systems 
Constitutional: Negative. Negative for fever, chills, diaphoresis, activity change, appetite change, fatigue and 
unexpected weight change. 
HENT: Negative. 
Eyes: Negative. 
Respiratory: Negative. Negative for shortness of breath and wheezing. 
Cardiovascular: Negative. Negative for chest pain and palpitations. 
Gastrointestinal: Negative. 
Endocrine: Negative. 
Skin: Positive for rash. 
 Perianal irritation as per hpi
Allergic/Immunologic: Negative. 
Neurological: Negative. 
Hematological: Negative. 
Psychiatric/Behavioral: Negative. 
All other systems reviewed and are negative.

Clinical Notes (continued)
This is a recurrent problem. The problem is unchanged. Pain location: perirectal area. The rash is characterized by 
dryness, itchiness and pain. He was exposed to nothing. Pertinent negatives include no anorexia, congestion, 
diarrhea, fatigue or fever. 
Back Pain
This is a new problem. The current episode started 1 to 4 weeks ago. The problem occurs intermittently. The problem 
has been gradually improving since onset. The pain is present in the lumbar spine (cervical). The pain is mild. The 
symptoms are aggravated by bending and position. Pertinent negatives include no fever. Risk factors include recent 
trauma (recent MVA). He has tried NSAIDs for the symptoms. The treatment provided moderate relief. 
The following portions of the patient's history were reviewed and updated as appropriate: allergies, current 
medications, past family history, past medical history, past social history, past surgical history and problem list.
Review of Systems 
Constitutional: Negative for fever, chills, diaphoresis, activity change, appetite change, fatigue and unexpected weight 
change. 
HENT: Negative. Negative for congestion. 
Eyes: Negative. 
Respiratory: Negative. 
Cardiovascular: Negative. 
Gastrointestinal: Negative. Negative for diarrhea and anorexia. 
Endocrine: Negative. 
Genitourinary: Negative. 
Musculoskeletal: Positive for back pain and arthralgias. Skin: Positive for rash. Objective:

kg/m2 | SpO2 98%
Physical Exam
Constitutional: He appears well-developed and well-nourished. No distress. 
HENT: 
Head: Normocephalic and atraumatic. 
Mouth/Throat: Oropharynx is clear and moist. 
Neck: Normal range of motion. Neck supple. 
Cardiovascular: Normal rate, regular rhythm and normal heart sounds. 
Pulmonary/Chest: Effort normal and breath sounds normal. 
Abdominal: Soft. He exhibits no distension. 
Genitourinary: 
Deferred 
Musculoskeletal: 
 Cervical back: He exhibits normal range of motion, no tenderness, no pain and no spasm. 
 Lumbar back: He exhibits normal range of motion, no pain and no spasm. 
Lymphadenopathy: 
 He has no cervical adenopathy. 
Skin: Skin is warm and dry. He is not diaphoretic.

Patient Summary as-of Visit (continued)
Clinical Notes
Subjective:
Patient ID: James R Kiper is a 39 yr/o male.
Sinus Problem
This is a recurrent problem. The current episode started more than 1 month ago. The problem has been waxing and 
waning since onset. There has been no fever. He is experiencing no pain. Associated symptoms include congestion, 
coughing, headaches, sinus pressure, sneezing and a sore throat. Pertinent negatives include no chills, diaphoresis, 
ear pain, hoarse voice, neck pain, shortness of breath or swollen glands. Treatments tried: benadryl. The treatment 
provided no relief.

Sign Up for MyChart
MyChart allows you to view portions of your electronic medical record. If you have a Norton Healthcare 
primary care physician, you can send messages to your doctor's office, view lab results and appointment 
information, medications, immunizations, allergies and more. You can even request appointments. To sign 
up, log on to https://mychart.nortonhealthcare.org. Once you are logged on, click on the Sign Up Now
link and you will access the new member sign-up page. Enter your MyChart Access Code exactly as it 
appears below to complete the sign-up process. If you do not sign up before the expiration date, you must 
request a new code.

authorized caregiver for another person and want proxy access to their MyChart account, please email the 
request to [EMAIL-979]. 
If you have other questions, you can email [EMAIL-980] or call [PHONE-981]. 
Remember, MyChart is NOT to be used for urgent needs. For medical emergencies, dial 911. After-Hours Instructions

Dear Mr. Kiper:
Thank you for visiting our office today. We understand sometimes you leave the office and 
then think of non-urgent questions you wished you would have thought to ask. Well, we’re here 
for you. Feel free to use the attached questionnaire to ask those questions via MyChart. A 
staff member or physician will be happy to answer any non-urgent questions you may have. If 
this is a medical emergency, please call 911 or go to the nearest Norton Healthcare 
emergency department. 
We are privileged to be your choice for medical care. Wishing you good health.

[DOB-6111] - Office Visit in Norton Community Medical Associates - Audubon West 200 (continued)
Clinical Notes (continued)
Subjective:
Patient ID: James R Kiper is a 39 yr/o male.
Abdominal Pain
This is a new problem. The current episode started 1 to 4 weeks ago. The onset quality is gradual. The problem 
occurs intermittently. The problem has been waxing and waning. The pain is located in the LUQ, LLQ and left flank. 
The pain is mild. The quality of the pain is aching and cramping. The abdominal pain does not radiate. Associated 
symptoms include arthralgias. Pertinent negatives include no anorexia, belching, constipation, fever, melena, 
myalgias, nausea, vomiting or weight loss. The pain is aggravated by palpation. The pain is relieved by nothing. 
Back Pain
This is a recurrent problem. The problem occurs daily. The problem has been waxing and waning since onset. The 
pain is present in the lumbar spine and sacro-iliac. The pain is moderate. Associated symptoms include abdominal 
pain. Pertinent negatives include no fever or weight loss. 
The following portions of the patient's history were reviewed and updated as appropriate: allergies, current 
medications, past family history, past medical history, past social history, past surgical history and problem list.
Review of Systems 
Constitutional: Negative for fever, chills, weight loss, diaphoresis, activity change, appetite change, fatigue and 
unexpected weight change. 
HENT: Negative. 
Eyes: Negative. 
Respiratory: Negative. 
Cardiovascular: Negative. 
Gastrointestinal: Positive for abdominal pain. Negative for nausea, vomiting, constipation, melena, abdominal 
distention and anorexia. 
Musculoskeletal: Positive for back pain and arthralgias. Negative for myalgias. 
Skin: Negative for color change, pallor and rash. 
Objective:
BP 118/78 mmHg | Pulse 98 | Ht 5' 8" (1.727 m) | Wt 152 lb (68.947 kg) | BMI 23.12 kg/m2 | SpO2 98%
Physical Exam
Constitutional: He is oriented to person, place, and time. He appears well-developed and well-nourished. No distress. 
HENT: 
Head: Normocephalic and atraumatic. 
Mouth/Throat: Oropharynx is clear and moist. 
Neck: Normal range of motion. 
Abnormal skin lesion on left neck

Dear DR DONOHUE,
Attached and/or below are the visit notes, diagnosis and treatment plan and recommended follow-up.
I saw JAMES R KIPER on March 25, 2021 for rash.
This is a 44 year old male who is being seen for a chief complaint of rash,located on the anus. This has been going on for almost 6
years. Has tried topical steroids, oral antifungals,topical antifungals, IMK, avoidance of any products or wipes in past. He does have a
history of hemorrhoids, he has seen his Gl they say it's possibly an allergic reaction to something. He has been tested for herpes and
wheat allergies. Bacterial swab performed in January 2021 and grew strep bacteria. Pt was given antibiotic ointment. He has been
applying this daily along with Vaseline without much relief. He has never been patch tested before. Pt has tried Gabapentin in the past
without much relief as well.
My impression and plan was the following:
1.Dermatitis Unspecified, Status:Inadequately Controlled
Counseling
Prescription: Diflucan 200 mg tablet PO
Other: Other (Free Text) - Discussed pt's symptoms in depth. Based on pt's history and his clinical exam today (erythematous patches
that have not improved with topical steroids/calcineurin inhibitors/antibiotics, oral antifungals, vaseline), would potentially favor a contact
dermatitis vs dysesthesia at this time,he has never been patch tested or biopsied before,would recommend that he get patch tested
and can try oral antifungals fnr nnw (difIncan POO mg weekly x4 weeks then ri/c) He reports benefit from using his sisters wash that he
believes is for yeast infections. Will reassess in 1-2 months, may need to biopsy if no improvement or no remarkable results from patch
testing.
He reports having been on gabapentin for something else (slipped disk) previously and tolerating poorly. May consider pregabalin if no
improvement with other means;.
Defer:Procedure to be performed at next visit - Patch testing; Reason to Defer - needs to schedule a separate appointment.
Cindy Owen, MD

Clinical Notes (continued)
Cough
This is a new problem. The current episode started in the past 7 days. The problem has been gradually worsening. 
The cough is non-productive. Associated symptoms include nasal congestion, postnasal drip and rhinorrhea. Pertinent 
negatives include no chest pain, chills, fever, headaches, sore throat, shortness of breath, sweats, weight loss or 
wheezing. The symptoms are aggravated by lying down. He has tried OTC cough suppressant for the symptoms. The 
treatment provided mild relief. His past medical history is significant for bronchitis. 
The following portions of the patient's history were reviewed and updated as appropriate: allergies, current 
medications, past family history, past medical history, past social history, past surgical history and problem list.
Review of Systems 
Constitutional: Negative for fever, chills, weight loss, diaphoresis, activity change, appetite change, fatigue and 
unexpected weight change. 
HENT: Positive for congestion, postnasal drip and rhinorrhea. Negative for sore throat. 
 Recurrent left cervical lymphadenopathy; painful
Respiratory: Positive for cough. Negative for shortness of breath and wheezing. 
Cardiovascular: Negative for chest pain. 
Neurological: Negative for headaches.

Left Ear: Hearing and external ear normal. 
Nose: Nose normal. Right sinus exhibits no maxillary sinus tenderness and no frontal sinus tenderness. Left sinus 
exhibits no maxillary sinus tenderness and no frontal sinus tenderness. 
Mouth/Throat: Uvula is midline and oropharynx is clear and moist. 
Neck: Normal range of motion. Neck supple. 
Cardiovascular: Normal rate and normal heart sounds.

Abdominal: Soft.

 He has cervical adenopathy.

 Left cervical: Superficial cervical adenopathy present. No deep cervical and no posterior cervical adenopathy 
present. 
Neurological: He is alert and oriented to person, place, and time.

Dear Kathryn A. Dunlap NMA APRN,
I would liketo give you an update after our visit today.In an effort to keep unnecessary paperwork from
being generated, I am sending a short synopsis of my note. I would be happy to forward you a complete
note upon request.
ASSESSMENT/S:
Laryngopharyngeal reflux (LPR), dysphagia, Cough, eustachian tube dysfunction, headache. Chronic
sinusitis, unspecified location, Postnasal drip, Perennial allergic rhinitis, thyroid nodule, and Hypothyroid
SUMMARY:
[DOB-6005] This is a very pleasant 46 year old who has been followed inthe past in regards to a 4 mm
right lower thyroid nodule as he has a history of hypothyroidism and has continued to do well on 100
meg of Synthroid. He most recently had a thyroid ultrasound in May of 2021 and this showed stable
findings with no change in his nodule. He also has a history of allergic rhinitis symptoms have continued
to be fairly well controlled with daily Claritin. He does report approximately one year of intermittent
swallowing issues especially with citrus food and pills. On exam he has no acute sinonasal inflammation
anteriorly or posteriorly on endoscopic exam, he has a stable leftward septal deviation, he does have
bilateral turbinate hypertrophy with mild to moderate allergic drainage, he has mild LPR changes on
posterior exam with no other source of his dysphagia noted. We discussed conservative measures in
regards to his acid reflux and otherwise we can see him back in one year to follow his progress or
sooner if needed. In the meantime he will begin omeprazole 20 mg daily.
I appreciate the opportunity to be involved with the care of this patient. Please feel free to contact me
directly with any questions (mobile [PHONE-1227]).
initial
Wes A. Allison, M.D.

Louisville, KY 40213

Dear Kathryn,
I would liketo give you an update after our visit today.In an effort to keep unnecessary paperwork from
being generated, I am sending a short synopsis of my note. I would be happy to forward you a complete
note upon request.
ASSESSMENT/S:
Cough, eustachian tube dysfunction, headache. Chronic sinusitis, unspecified location. Postnasal drip,
Perennial allergic rhinitis, thyroid nodule, and Hypothyroid
SUMMARY:
5-17-21 This is a very pleasant 45 year old who we are following in regards to a 4 mm right lower thyroid
nodule as he has a history of hypothyroidism and has been doing well on 100 meg of Synthroid over the
last 12 years. We were planning on a repeat thyroid ultrasound in October 2020 though he has not had
the ultrasound performed as of yet. He has been doing well on Claritin daily and still wishes to hold off
on immunotherapy as testing showed moderate allergies on testing in 2019. On exam he has no acute
sinonasal inflammation anteriorly, his throat is clear and his thyroid is normal to palpation. We will obtain
an updated thyroid ultrasound at Norton for continuity and we can call him with these results. As long as
the nodule is stable, we will not necessarily need to continue to follow it any further. We can follow with
him in one year in regards to his allergies.
I appreciate the opportunity to be involved with the care of this patient. Please feel free to contact me
with any questions - cell[PHONE-1247].

Dear Mr. Kiper:
Thank you for visiting our office today. We understand sometimes you leave the office and 
then think of non-urgent questions you wish you had asked. Well, we’re here for you. Feel free

will take the time to complete it. We value what you have to say and use your feedback to 
improve our patient care services here at Norton Healthcare. Thank you!
If this is a medical emergency, please call 911 or go to the nearest Norton Healthcare 
emergency department. 
We are privileged to be your choice for medical care. Wishing you good health.

Clinical Notes (continued)
The pain is present in the left shoulder. This is a new problem. The current episode started 1 to 4 weeks ago. History 
of extremity trauma: overuse at work but no injury. The problem occurs daily. The problem has been unchanged. The 
quality of the pain is described as aching. The pain is moderate. Associated symptoms include joint swelling, a limited 
range of motion and stiffness. Pertinent negatives include no numbness or tingling. The symptoms are aggravated by 
activity. He has tried NSAIDS and rest for the symptoms. The treatment provided mild relief. Family history does not 
include gout or rheumatoid arthritis. There is no history of osteoarthritis. 
The following portions of the patient's history were reviewed and updated as appropriate: allergies, current 
medications, past family history, past medical history, past social history, past surgical history and problem list.
Review of Systems 
Constitutional: Negative for chills, diaphoresis, activity change, appetite change, fatigue and unexpected weight 
change. 
Musculoskeletal: Positive for myalgias, joint swelling, arthralgias and stiffness. Neurological: Negative for tingling and numbness. 
Objective:
BP 104/60 mmHg | Pulse 71 | Ht 5' 8" (1.727 m) | Wt 153 lb (69.4 kg) | BMI 23.27 kg/m2 | SpO2 98%
Physical Exam
Constitutional: He is oriented to person, place, and time. He appears well-developed and well-nourished. No distress. 
HENT: 
Head: Normocephalic. 
Cardiovascular: Normal rate, regular rhythm and normal heart sounds. 
Pulmonary/Chest: Effort normal and breath sounds normal. 
Abdominal: Soft. 
Musculoskeletal: 
 Left shoulder: He exhibits decreased range of motion, tenderness, pain, spasm and decreased strength. He 
exhibits no bony tenderness, no swelling, no deformity and normal pulse. 
Neurological: He is alert and oriented to person, place, and time. 
Skin: He is not diaphoretic. 
Nursing note and vitals reviewed.
Assessment and Plan:
James was seen today for shoulder pain.
Diagnoses and all orders for this visit:
Rotator cuff strain, left, initial encounter

-Naproxen-Esomeprazole (VIMOVO) 500-20 MG TB DR; Take 1 tablet by mouth Twice a Day

Patient Instructions
Rotator Cuff Injury
WHAT YOU SHOULD KNOW:
A rotator cuff injury is damage to the muscles or tendons of your rotator cuff. The rotator cuff is made up of a group of 
muscles and tendons that hold the shoulder joint in place. The damage may include stretching of your muscle or tears 
in the tendons. It may also include inflammation of the bursa (small sack of fluid around the joint).
INSTRUCTIONS: · Acetaminophen: This medicine decreases pain. Acetaminophen is available without a doctor's order. Ask how 
much to take and how often to take it. Follow directions. Acetaminophen can cause liver damage if not taken 
correctly. · NSAIDs: These medicines decrease swelling, pain, and fever. NSAIDs are available without a doctor's order. 
Ask your primary healthcare provider which medicine is right for you. Ask how much to take and when to take it. 
Take as directed. NSAIDs can cause stomach bleeding or kidney problems if not taken correctly. · Pain medicine: You may be given a prescription medicine to decrease pain. Do not wait until the pain is severe 
before you take this medicine. · Steroids: This medicine may be injected into the rotator cuff area to decrease inflammation and pain. · Take your medicine as directed. Call your primary healthcare provider if you think your medicine is not helping 
or if you have side effects. Tell him if you are allergic to any medicine. Keep a list of the medicines, vitamins, and 
herbs you take. Include the amounts, and when and why you take them. Bring the list or the pill bottles to followup visits. Carry your medicine list with you in case of an emergency.
Follow up with your primary healthcare provider or orthopedist as directed: Write down your questions so you 
remember to ask them during your visits.

Patient Instructions (continued)
Physical therapy: A physical therapist can teach you exercises to help improve movement and strength, and to 
decrease pain. These exercises may help you go back to your usual activities or return to playing sports. 
Self-care: · Rest: Rest may help your shoulder heal. Overuse of your shoulder can make your injury worse. Avoid heavy 
lifting, using your arms over your head, or any other activity that makes the pain worse. · Put ice or heat on your shoulder: Use ice on your shoulder every few hours for the first several days. This 
may help decrease pain and swelling. After the first several days, a heating pad may help relax the muscles in 
your shoulder.
Contact your primary healthcare provider or orthopedist if: · The pain in your shoulder or arm is not improving, or is worse than before you started treatment. · You have new pain in your neck. · You have a fever. · You have questions or concerns about your condition or care.
Return to the emergency department if: · You suddenly cannot move your arm. · You have severe stomach or back pain, are vomiting blood, or have black bowel movements.

April 27, 2016 
Dear Mr. Kiper:
Thank you for visiting our office today. We understand sometimes you leave the office and 
then think of non-urgent questions you wish you had asked. Well, we’re here for you. Feel free 
to use the attached questionnaire to ask those questions via MyChart. A staff member or 
physician will be happy to answer any non-urgent questions you may have. 
Your feedback is very important to us! You may receive a survey in the mail, and we hope you 
will take the time to complete it. We value what you have to say and use your feedback to 
improve our patient care services here at Norton Healthcare. Thank you!
If this is a medical emergency, please call 911 or go to the nearest Norton Healthcare 
emergency department. 
We are privileged to be your choice for medical care. Wishing you good health.

[DOB-1454] - Office Visit in Norton Community Medical Associates - Audubon West 200 (continued)
Clinical Notes (continued)
HPI Comments: Here to follow up from recent visit for left shoulder pain thought to be due to rotator cuff strain. 
At last visit we did xrays which were unremarkable.
He has been going to physical therapy but has only had a few visits. Using NSaids with modest relief of symptoms. 
Concerned about returning to work; job occasionally requires heavy lifting. Feels he is able to do job as long as he 
does not have to lift heavy loads overhead. If he does not return to work he will lose insurance which concerns him. 
Shoulder Pain
The pain is present in the neck and left shoulder. History of extremity trauma: repetiive use at work. The problem 
occurs daily. The problem has been unchanged. The quality of the pain is described as aching. The pain is moderate. 
Associated symptoms include a limited range of motion and stiffness. Pertinent negatives include no fever, joint 
locking, joint swelling, numbness or tingling. Treatments tried: physical therapy, nsaids. The treatment provided mild 
relief. 
The following portions of the patient's history were reviewed and updated as appropriate: allergies, current 
medications, past family history, past medical history, past social history, past surgical history and problem list.
Review of Systems 
Constitutional: Negative for fever, chills, diaphoresis, activity change, appetite change, fatigue and unexpected weight 
change. 
Musculoskeletal: Positive for myalgias, arthralgias, stiffness, neck pain and neck stiffness. Neurological: Negative for tingling and numbness. 
Objective:
BP 120/62 mmHg | Pulse 87 | Ht 5' 8" (1.727 m) | Wt 154 lb (69.854 kg) | BMI 23.42 kg/m2 | SpO2 99%
Physical Exam
Constitutional: He is oriented to person, place, and time. He appears well-developed and well-nourished. No distress. 
HENT: 
Head: Normocephalic and atraumatic. 
Neck: Normal range of motion. 
Cardiovascular: Normal rate and regular rhythm. 
Pulmonary/Chest: Effort normal. No respiratory distress. 
Musculoskeletal: 
 Left shoulder: He exhibits pain, spasm and decreased strength. He exhibits no bony tenderness. 
Neurological: He is alert and oriented to person, place, and time. 
Skin: He is not diaphoretic. 
Nursing note and vitals reviewed.
Assessment and Plan:
James was seen today for follow-up.
Diagnoses and all orders for this visit:
Left shoulder pain

Sign Up for MyChart
MyChart allows you to view portions of your electronic medical record. If you have a Norton Healthcare 
primary care physician, you can send messages to your doctor's office, view lab results and appointment 
information, medications, immunizations, allergies and more. You can even request appointments. To sign

Dear Mr. Kiper:
Thank you for visiting our office today. We understand sometimes you leave the office and 
then think of non-urgent questions you wish you had asked. Well, we’re here for you. Feel free

improve our patient care services here at Norton Healthcare. Thank you!
