Patient Summary as-of Visit (continued)
Clinical Notes
CHIEF COMPLAINT
Chief Complaint
Patient presents with
• Neck Pain
NEEDS REFERRAL FOR NEUROLOGIST
• Shoulder Pain
SUBJECTIVE
James R Kiper is a 40 yr/o male who presents to the office with features of bilateral cervical radiculitis. Left arm 
shoulder and hand have pain in the neuropathic distribution. Right arm and forearm have similar type of involvement. 
This apparently has occurred since recent MVA. The patient can reproduce brachial neuronitis features with flexion of 
the neck
ROS
Review of Systems - General ROS: negative
Respiratory ROS: no cough, shortness of breath, or wheezing
Cardiovascular ROS: no chest pain or dyspnea on exertion
Neurological ROS: negative for - confusion, dizziness, numbness/tingling, or weakness
MEDICATIONS
Current Outpatient Prescriptions
Medication Sig Dispense Refill • ibuprofen (ADVIL,MOTRIN) 800 MG 
tablet • levothyroxine (SYNTHROID, 
LEVOTHROID) 100 MCG tablet
TAKE ONE TABLET BY 
MOUTH DAILY
90 tablet 0
• methocarbamol (ROBAXIN) 750 MG 
tablet
5
• Naproxen-Esomeprazole (VIMOVO) 
500-20 MG TB DR
Take 1 tablet by mouth 
Twice a Day
60 tablet 2

HIGH FIELD
OPEN MRI
\ I
The Leaderin Advanced Patient-Friendly Imaging
OPEN MRI, LLC |Ph:[PHONE-1608] |FAX:[PHONE-1609]
7807 Shelbyville Road, Louisville, KY 40222 5227 Dixie Highway, Louisville, KY 40216
Exam: Cervical Spine MRI
DATE OF EXAM: [DOB-5621]
Referring Physician: Donohue,
Terrence M.D.
Patient Name: KIPER, JAMES P
DOB: [DOB-7230]
Patient No: 224014
Accession #: 506239
Interpreting Rad:Dubose, Beth M.D.
CLINICAL HISTORY: 40 year-old male with neck pain and bilateral upper extremity radiculopathy since MVA
7/12/15. Evaluate for cervical spine abnormality.
TECHNIQUE: Imaging is performed on a 1.5 Tesla system. Multiple weightings including T1 and T2 were
acquired in multiple imaging planes.
FINDINGS: The C1-T2 vertebrae are visualized. The vertebral bodies are normal in height and signal intensity.
The cervical spinal cord is normal in morphology and signal intensity. Imaged portions of the cerebellum, pons, and
brainstem are unremarkable.
C2-3: Unremarkable.
C3-4: There is a minimal central disc bulge. The spinal canal and neural foramina are patent.
C4-5, C5-6: Unremarkable.
C6-7: There is 1-2 mm left paracentral disc bulging. The spinal canal and neural foramina are patent.
C7-T1, T1-T2: Unremarkable.
The surrounding soft tissues, as imaged are unremarkable.
IMPRESSION:
1. At C3-4, there is minimal central disc bulging, and at C6-7. There is 1-2 mm left paracentral disc bulging. Spinal
canal and neural foramina are patent.
2. No other bulge or herniation.
Thank you for referring KIPER, JAMES P to High Field and Open MRI.
If you have any questions or would like to speak to one of^
our Radiologists regarding this exam, please call (502)

Clinical Notes
CHIEF COMPLAINT
Chief Complaint
Patient presents with
• Back Pain
MRI RESULTS, BULGING DISKS
SUBJECTIVE
James R Kiper is a 40 yr/o male who presents today in follow-up of features consistent with bilateral cervical 
radiculitis. The patient principally has left arm and shoulder as well as hand discomfort in the neuropathic distribution 
right arm is not his problem. These features have occurred since recent MVA.
Recent MRI demonstrates a bulging disc
Patient describes cervical pain with radiculitis to be mild to moderate at this time.
ROS
Review of Systems - General ROS: negative
Respiratory ROS: no cough, shortness of breath, or wheezing
Cardiovascular ROS: no chest pain or dyspnea on exertion
Neurological ROS: negative for - confusion, dizziness, numbness/tingling, or weakness

tablet

500-20 MG TB DR

Lymphatic: No lymphadenopathy noted.

tendon reflexes and strength in the upper extremity demonstrates good strength with bilateral symmetry

Clinical Notes (continued)
HPI 
Palpitations: Patient has had Hashimoto's thyroiditis for a number of years and resulting hypothyroidism. He is on 
Synthroid 100 micrograms daily and in the last few weeks has noted some increasing palpitations. They mainly occur 
while at rest and not with exercise. He is not having any shortness of breath, chest pain, or edema. Does not have 
any known cardiac or renal disease. He denies any excessive stress or worry. He does consume about 250-350 
milligrams of caffeine per day. He eats a vegetarian diet.
The following portions of the patient's history were reviewed and updated as appropriate: allergies, current 
medications, past family history, past medical history, past social history, past surgical history and problem list.
Review of Systems 
Respiratory: Negative. 
Cardiovascular: Positive for palpitations. Negative for chest pain and leg swelling. 
Objective:
BP 102/60 (BP Location: Left arm, Orthostatic Position: Sitting) Pulse 79 Ht 5' 8" (1.727 m) Wt 154 lb (69.9 kg) 
SpO2 98% BMI 23.42 kg/m2
Physical Exam
Constitutional: He appears well-developed and well-nourished. No distress. 
HENT: 
Head: Normocephalic and atraumatic. 
Right Ear: External ear normal. 
Left Ear: External ear normal. 
Nose: Nose normal. 
Mouth/Throat: Oropharynx is clear and moist. 
Neck: Normal range of motion. Neck supple. No tracheal deviation present. No thyromegaly present. 
Cardiovascular: Normal rate, regular rhythm, normal heart sounds and intact distal pulses. 
No murmur heard.
Pulmonary/Chest: Effort normal and breath sounds normal. No respiratory distress. He has no wheezes. 
Abdominal: Soft. Bowel sounds are normal. He exhibits no distension. There is no tenderness. 
Skin: He is not diaphoretic. 
Psychiatric: He has a normal mood and affect. His behavior is normal. 
Nursing note and vitals reviewed.
Assessment and Plan:
James was seen today for office visit.

[DOB-5390] - Office Visit in Norton Community Medical Associates - Audubon West 200 (continued)
Clinical Notes (continued)
Palpitations have improved; he admits to consuming significant amounts of caffeine at that time. 
He was also found to be slightly iron deficient; eats a vegetarian diet. Had tried oral iron supplementation but could not 
tolerate due to constipation. 
Lab Results
 Component Value Date 
 FERRITIN 17 (L) [DOB-1842] 
Unfortunately had a bout of hemorrhoids and significant rectal pain prompting him to present to ICC and later the ER. 
He had CT scan which was unremarkable. Has since seen surgery who performed a thrombectomy and he has had 
significant improvement in rectal pain. He does have a history of anal fissures and is due to follow up with surgery later 
this week. 
The following portions of the patient's history were reviewed and updated as appropriate: allergies, current 
medications, past family history, past medical history, past social history, past surgical history and problem list.
Review of Systems 
Constitutional: Negative for activity change, appetite change, chills, diaphoresis, fatigue, fever and unexpected weight 
change. 
Respiratory: Negative for shortness of breath and wheezing. 
Cardiovascular: Negative for chest pain, palpitations and leg swelling. 
Gastrointestinal: Positive for anal bleeding and rectal pain. Objective:
BP 110/70 (BP Location: Right arm) Ht 5' 8" (1.727 m) Wt 149 lb (67.6 kg) BMI 22.66 kg/m2
Physical Exam
Constitutional: He is oriented to person, place, and time. He appears well-developed and well-nourished. No distress. 
HENT: 
Head: Normocephalic and atraumatic. 
Cardiovascular: Normal rate, regular rhythm and normal heart sounds. 
Pulmonary/Chest: Effort normal and breath sounds normal. 
Neurological: He is alert and oriented to person, place, and time. 
Skin: Skin is warm and dry. He is not diaphoretic. 
Nursing note and vitals reviewed.
Assessment and Plan:
James was seen today for follow-up.
Diagnoses and all orders for this visit:
Hypothyroidism due to Hashimoto's thyroiditis

Daily iron needs: · Males:

¨ 14 to 18 years old: 11 mg
¨ 19 years and older: 8 mg

Foods that contain iron: · Meat, fish, and poultry are good sources of iron. They contain heme iron, a form of iron that your body absorbs 
very well. Fruit, vegetables, eggs, and grains such as pasta, rice, and cereal also contain iron. They contain 
nonheme iron, a form of iron that is not absorbed as well as heme iron. You can absorb more iron from these 
foods by eating a food that is high in vitamin C at the same time. You can also absorb more nonheme iron by 
eating a food from the meat, fish, and poultry group at the same time. · Fish and shellfish contain some mercury, a metal that can be harmful to the body. Children and unborn babies 
are at higher risk for harm caused by mercury. Children and pregnant women should avoid eating fish high in 
mercury, such as shark and swordfish. They should also eat only fish that are lower in mercury, such as salmon, 
canned light tuna, and catfish. Limit the amount of low-mercury fish and shellfish you eat to less than 12 ounces 
per week.
Iron-rich foods: · Foods that contain 2 mg or more per serving: 
¨ 3 ounces of cooked beef (chuck, eye of round) or cooked turkey (dark meat)

· Foods that contain 1 to 2 mg per serving:

¨ 3 ounces of pork

¨ 3 ounces of light tuna
¨ ½ cup of seedless, packed raisins
¨ 1 slice of whole-wheat or white bread
Good sources of vitamin C: Eat a serving of vitamin C with any iron-rich food to help your body absorb more iron. 
The following fruits and vegetables are good sources of vitamin C: · 1 cup of fresh orange juice (124 mg) or pink grapefruit juice (83 mg) · 1 cup of strawberries (106 mg) · 1 cup of diced cantaloupe (68 mg) · 1 cup of sweet yellow pepper (283 mg) · 1 cup of fresh, boiled broccoli (116 mg) or cooked brussels sprouts (97 mg) · 1 cup of kale (53 mg) · 1 cup of tomato juice (45 mg)
Other guidelines to follow: · Tea and coffee can decrease the amount of iron that your body absorbs from iron-rich foods. Drink coffee and 
tea separately from meals that contain iron-rich foods. · Children over the age of 1 year only need about 24 ounces of cow's milk each day. When children drink too 
much milk, they may eat fewer iron-rich foods. This may cause them to have a low level of iron in their blood.
Risks: If you do not eat iron-rich foods and vitamin C every day, you may have low blood iron levels. This may lead to 
iron deficiency anemia, especially during periods when your body needs extra iron. Iron deficiency anemia may cause 
problems with your child's growth and development. If you have iron deficiency anemia, you may develop other health

Patient Instructions
Iron Rich Diet
WHAT YOU NEED TO KNOW:
An iron-rich diet includes foods that are good sources of iron. People need extra iron during childhood, adolescence 
(teenage years), and pregnancy. Iron is a mineral that your body needs to make hemoglobin. Hemoglobin is part of 
your blood and helps carry oxygen from your lungs to the rest of your body. You may need to follow this diet to treat or 
prevent a low blood iron level or iron deficiency anemia. 
DISCHARGE INSTRUCTIONS:
Daily iron needs:

Patient Summary as-of Visit (continued)
Clinical Notes
Subjective:
Patient ID: James R Kiper is a 40 yr/o male.
Patient presents today today with complaints of left shoulder pain. Chronic recurrent issue since last year. Was doing 
physical therapy and showed improvement. However, patient noted a return of symptoms after starting back at work 
with Jefferson county public schools. His job entails lifting heavy boxes of canned goods. 
Shoulder Pain The pain is present in the left shoulder. This is a new problem. The current episode started more than 1 month ago. 
There has been no history of extremity trauma. The problem occurs daily. The problem has been unchanged. The 
quality of the pain is described as aching and sharp. Associated symptoms include a limited range of motion. Pertinent 
negatives include no fever, inability to bear weight, joint swelling, numbness, stiffness or tingling. The symptoms are 
aggravated by activity. He has tried rest for the symptoms. The treatment provided mild relief.

Clinical Notes
Subjective:
Patient ID: James R Kiper is a 40 yr/o male here for evaluation of right shoulder pain. He has been dealing with left 
shoulder pain for some time, has an appointment with Ortho on 10/10/16, and tried to go back to work yesterday. 
While lifting a pan out of water, he felt a pull and a sharp pain in his right upper back and shoulder. His left shoulder is 
unchanged.

Clinical Notes (continued)
Shoulder Pain The pain is present in the neck, back and right shoulder. This is a new problem. The current episode started 
yesterday. There has been a history of trauma. The problem occurs constantly. The problem has been unchanged. 
The quality of the pain is described as aching. The pain is moderate. Associated symptoms include an inability to bear 
weight (pain with weight bearing) and a limited range of motion (pain with overhead lifting). Pertinent negatives include 
no fever, itching, joint locking, joint swelling, numbness, stiffness or tingling. The symptoms are aggravated by activity. 
He has tried NSAIDS (muscle relaxants) for the symptoms. The treatment provided moderate relief. Family history 
does not include gout or rheumatoid arthritis. There is no history of diabetes, gout, osteoarthritis or rheumatoid 
arthritis. 
The following portions of the patient's history were reviewed and updated as appropriate: allergies, current 
medications, past family history, past medical history, past social history, past surgical history and problem list.
Review of Systems 
Constitutional: Negative for activity change, appetite change, chills, fatigue, fever and unexpected weight change. 
Respiratory: Negative for cough, shortness of breath and wheezing. 
Cardiovascular: Negative for chest pain and palpitations. 
Musculoskeletal: Positive for arthralgias (left shoulder, at baseline), back pain (right upper back), myalgias (right 
shoulder muscles) and neck pain (right side). Negative for gout and stiffness. 
Skin: Negative for itching. 
Neurological: Negative for tingling and numbness. 
Objective:
BP 112/60 (BP Location: Right arm) Ht 5' 8" (1.727 m) Wt 146 lb (66.2 kg) BMI 22.2 kg/m2
Physical Exam
Constitutional: He appears well-developed and well-nourished. 
Neck: Normal range of motion. 
Cardiovascular: Normal rate, regular rhythm and normal heart sounds. 
No murmur heard.
Pulmonary/Chest: Effort normal and breath sounds normal. He has no wheezes. 
Musculoskeletal: He exhibits tenderness (right latissimus and trapezius muscles tender to palpation, with 
notable spasm). He exhibits no edema or deformity. 
Assessment and Plan:
James was seen today for shoulder pain.
Diagnoses and all orders for this visit:
Right shoulder strain, initial encounter

Take all your medications as directed
We encourage you to follow these recommendations:
 1. Exercise regularly (3 hours per week minimum) and normalize your weight (BMI of less than 30).
2. See the eye doctor every two years after you turn 40 and the dentist every 6 months.
 3. Take your medications regularly as directed, and see your physician every 3-6 months ( or more often if 
instructed by doctor) for regular follow-up.
 4. We want your blood pressure to run below 140/90.
5. Avoid all tobacco use and any exposure to second hand smoke at home or work.
6. Please get a flu shot every fall, if not contraindicated.
7. Wear seatbelts whenever riding in a car or truck.
8. Make sure you keep up with age appropriate screenings and immunizations (Colonoscopy after age 50, PSA 
annually for men 50-75), mammograms annually for women 40-70, Pap smears every 2 years for women 21-65, 
Tetanus booster every 10 years, Pneumonia shot after age 50, shingles shot after age 50)

Clinical Notes
Subjective:
Patient ID: James R Kiper is a 40 yr/o male.
HPI Comments: Hypothyroidism
James R Kiper is a 40 yr/o male who presents for follow up of hypothyroidism. Current symptoms: none . Patient 
denies change in energy level, diarrhea, heat / cold intolerance, nervousness, palpitations and weight changes. 
Symptoms have been basically asymptomatic. Recent tsh was elevated and so synthroid dosage was increased; due 
to recheck today

Sign Up for MyChart
MyChart allows you to view portions of your electronic medical record. If you have a Norton Healthcare 
primary care physician, you can send messages to your doctor's office, view lab results and appointment 
information, medications, immunizations, allergies and more. You can even request appointments. To sign 
up, log on to https://mychart.nortonhealthcare.org. Once you are logged on, click on the Sign Up Now
link and you will access the new member sign-up page. Enter your MyChart Access Code exactly as it 
appears below to complete the sign-up process. If you do not sign up before the expiration date, you must 
request a new code.

Patient Summary as-of Visit (continued)
Clinical Notes
CHIEF COMPLAINT
Chief Complaint
Patient presents with
• Shoulder Pain
follow up on bilateral shoulder pain, patient has seen katie and anne for these issues 
SUBJECTIVE
James R Kiper is a 40 yr/o male who presents to the office for follow-up of left greater than right shoulder pain. Review 
of patient's MRI of the left shoulder demonstrated that he had a 10 percent left infraspinatus muscle tear. His pain and 
discomfort seems to be well localized over this area. The patient has difficulty raising his arms and the motion of 
picking up cafeteria trays and placing them in the right.
The patient has a history of Hashimoto's thyroiditis. His TSH was 4.70 on 8/23/16. The patient also has iron deficiency. 
Previously was a high consumer of caffeine as well. The patient had external thrombosed hemorrhoids this has 
improved. Repeat TSH was 0.11 his free T4 was 1.87 these indicate a mild hyperthyroid status
External hemorrhoids, hypothyroidism and iron deficiency remains stable.
ROS
Review of Systems - General ROS: negative

SUBJECTIVE
James R Kiper is a 40 yr/o male who presents to the office for follow-up of left greater than right shoulder pain. Review 
of patient's MRI of the left shoulder demonstrated that he had a 10 percent left infraspinatus muscle tear. His pain and 
discomfort seems to be well localized over this area. The patient has difficulty raising his arms and the motion of 
picking up cafeteria trays and placing them in the right.
The patient has a history of Hashimoto's thyroiditis. His TSH was 4.70 on 8/23/16. The patient also has iron deficiency.

Clinical Notes
CHIEF COMPLAINT
Chief Complaint
Patient presents with
• Shoulder Pain
FOLLOW UP 
SUBJECTIVE
James R Kiper is a 40 yr/o male who presents to the office with complaints of continued left shoulder pain. Patient also 
has right shoulder pain. MRI of the left shoulder demonstrates a 10 percent left infraspinatus muscle tear. He has 
decrease range of motion. He also has external hemorrhoids which gives him some degree of tenesmus and rectal 
pain. Times is constipated. He has had iron deficiency anemia and hypothyroidism these conditions remained stable
ROS

Respiratory ROS: no cough, shortness of breath, or wheezing
Cardiovascular ROS: no chest pain or dyspnea on exertion
Neurological ROS: negative for - confusion, dizziness, numbness/tingling, or weakness

Sign Up for MyChart
MyChart allows you to view portions of your electronic medical record. If you have a Norton Healthcare 
primary care physician, you can send messages to your doctor's office, view lab results and appointment 
information, medications, immunizations, allergies and more. You can even request appointments. To sign 
up, log on to https://mychart.nortonhealthcare.org. Once you are logged on, click on the Sign Up Now
link and you will access the new member sign-up page. Enter your MyChart Access Code exactly as it 
appears below to complete the sign-up process. If you do not sign up before the expiration date, you must 
request a new code.

Clinical Notes
CHIEF COMPLAINT
Chief Complaint
Patient presents with
• Hand Pain
PT STATES HIS RIGHT HAND IS SWELLING AND PAINFUL
SUBJECTIVE
James R Kiper is a 40 yr/o male who presents to the office with complaints of right hand pain this was at the second 
and third metacarpal phalangeal joint. This occurred after using a drill. Patient relates she took a few antiinflammatories currently there is no heat warmth or synovitis.

Respiratory ROS: no cough, shortness of breath, or wheezing

February 14, 2017 
Dear Mr. Kiper:
Thank you for visiting our office today. We understand sometimes you leave the office and 
then think of non-urgent questions you wish you had asked. Well, we’re here for you. Feel free 
to use the attached questionnaire to ask those questions via MyChart. A staff member or 
provider will be happy to answer any non-urgent questions you may have. 
Your feedback is very important to us! You may receive a survey in the mail, and we hope you 
will take the time to complete it. We value what you have to say and use your feedback to 
improve our patient care services here at Norton Healthcare. Thank you!
If this is a medical emergency, please call 911 or go to the nearest Norton Healthcare 
emergency department. 
We are privileged to be your choice for medical care. Wishing you good health.

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Patient presents with
• Abdominal Pain
PT STATES HE IS HAVING SOME TENDERNESS IN HIS LOWER ABDOMINAL
• Testicle Pain
SUBJECTIVE
James R Kiper is a 40 yr/o male who presents to the office with lower abdominal pain as well as testicular pain this 
pain has been intermittently occurring for several weeks. Hypothyroidism remained stable. The patient complains of 
multiple allergies and will be referred to ear nose and throat. I suspect patient may need hyposensitization testing.

Instructions
o [DOB-4673] This is a very pleasant 40 year old who returns reporting worsening allergies over the past year despite trials
of Flonase and Zyrtec. He reports nasal trauma 2 months ago with a possible nasal injury. On exam his nose is midline
with no evidence of recent trauma, he continues to have a stable septal deviation to the left with allergic nasal findings
posteriorly and no acute inflammation on endoscopy. He is agreeable allergy workup and I can see him after this is
completed. We'll continue a daily spray and antihistamine regimen otherwise.
Correspondence
o Consult letter (Kristopher Fannin NMA MD) - [DOB-4672]

Patient Instructions
Asthma
WHAT YOU NEED TO KNOW:
What is asthma? Asthma is a lung disease that makes breathing difficult. Chronic inflammation and reactions to 
triggers narrow the airways in the lungs. Asthma can become life-threatening if it is not managed. 
What is cough-variant asthma? Cough-variant asthma is a type of asthma with symptoms of a recurrent dry cough. 
A dry cough may be your only symptom or you may also have chest tightness. These symptoms may be caused by 
exercise or exposure to odors, allergens, or respiratory tract infections. Cough-variant asthma is treated the same way 
as typical asthma. 
What are the signs and symptoms of asthma? · Coughing · Wheezing · Shortness of breath · Chest tightness
What may trigger an asthma attack?

[DOB-4594] - Office Visit in Norton Community Medical Associates - Audubon West 200 (continued)
Patient Instructions (continued) · A cold, the flu, or a sinus infection · Exercise · Weather changes, especially cold, dry air · Smoking or secondhand smoke
· Fumes from chemicals, dust, air pollution, or other small particles in the air · Pets, pollen, dust mites, or cockroaches
How is asthma diagnosed? Your healthcare provider will ask how often you have symptoms and what makes them 
worse. Tell him if you have trouble sleeping, exercising, or doing other activities from shortness of breath. He will ask 
about your allergies and past colds, and if anyone in your family has allergies or asthma. Tell your healthcare provider 
about medicines you take, including over-the-counter drugs and herbal supplements. You may need an x-ray to check 
for lung problems, or a lung function test.
How is asthma treated? · Medicines decrease inflammation, open airways, and make it easier to breathe. Medicines may be inhaled, 
taken as a pill, or injected. Short-term medicines relieve your symptoms quickly. Long-term medicines are used 
to prevent future attacks. You may also need medicine to help control your allergies. · Allergy testing may find allergies that trigger an asthma attack. You may need allergy shots or medicine to

[DOB-4593] - Office Visit in Norton Community Medical Associates - Audubon West 200 (continued)
Patient Instructions (continued)
control allergies that make your asthma worse.
How can I manage my symptoms and prevent future attacks? · Follow your Asthma Action Plan (AAP). This is a written plan that you and your healthcare provider create. It 
explains which medicine you need and when to change doses if necessary. It also explains how you can monitor 
symptoms and use a peak flow meter. The meter measures how well your lungs are working. · Manage other health conditions , such as allergies, acid reflux, and sleep apnea. · Identify and avoid triggers. These may include pets, dust mites, mold, and cockroaches. · Do not smoke or be around others who smoke. Nicotine and other chemicals in cigarettes and cigars can 
cause lung damage. Ask your healthcare provider for information if you currently smoke and need help to quit. Ecigarettes or smokeless tobacco still contain nicotine. Talk to your healthcare provider before you use these 
products. · Ask about the flu vaccine. The flu can make your asthma worse. You may need a yearly flu shot.
When should I seek immediate care? · You have severe shortness of breath. · Your lips or nails turn blue or gray. · The skin around your neck and ribs pulls in with each breath. · You have shortness of breath, even after you take your short-term medicine as directed. · Your peak flow numbers are in the red zone of your AAP.
When should I contact my healthcare provider? · You run out of medicine before your next refill is due. · Your symptoms get worse. · You need to take more medicine than usual to control your symptoms. · You have questions or concerns about your condition or care.
CARE AGREEMENT:

May 15, 2017 
Dear Mr. Kiper:
Thank you for visiting our office today. We understand sometimes you leave the office and 
then think of non-urgent questions you wish you had asked. Well, we’re here for you. Feel free 
to use the attached questionnaire to ask those questions via MyNortonChart. A staff member 
or provider will be happy to answer any non-urgent questions you may have. 
Your feedback is very important to us! You may receive a survey in the mail, and we hope you 
will take the time to complete it. We value what you have to say and use your feedback to 
improve our patient care services here at Norton Healthcare. Thank you!
If this is a medical emergency, please call 911 or go to the nearest Norton Healthcare 
emergency department. 
We are privileged to be your choice for medical care. Wishing you good health.

Patient Instructions
Swollen Joint
WHAT YOU NEED TO KNOW:
What do I need to know about joint swelling? Joint swelling may occur in one or more joints. You may have other 
symptoms, such as pain, tenderness, or stiffness. A swollen joint may be caused by a variety of conditions such as 
arthritis, pseudogout, gout, tendinitis, or injury.
How is joint swelling diagnosed? Your healthcare provider will ask about your symptoms. He will examine your 
joint and check how well it moves in different directions. He may do blood tests or x-rays to find the cause of the 
swelling. He may also remove fluid from your joint and send it to a lab for tests.

[DOB-2693] - Office Visit in Norton Community Medical Associates - Audubon West 200 (continued)
Patient Instructions (continued)
How is joint swelling treated? Treatment depends on the cause of your swollen joint. Your healthcare provider may 
recommend any of the following: · Rest your swollen joint. Avoid activities that make the swelling or pain worse. You may need to avoid putting 
weight on your joint while you have pain. Crutches or a walker can be used to avoid putting weight on joints in 
your lower body. · Apply ice on your swollen joint for 15 to 20 minutes every hour or as directed. Use an ice pack, or put crushed 
ice in a plastic bag. Cover it with a towel. Ice helps prevent tissue damage and decreases swelling and pain. · Apply heat on your swollen joint for 20 to 30 minutes every 2 hours for as many days as directed. Heat helps 
decrease pain. · Elevate your swollen joint above the level of your heart as often as you can. This will help decrease swelling 
and pain. Prop your joint on pillows or blankets to keep it elevated comfortably. · NSAIDs , such as ibuprofen, help decrease swelling, pain, and fever. This medicine is available with or without a 
doctor's order. NSAIDs can cause stomach bleeding or kidney problems in certain people. If you take blood 
thinner medicine, always ask your healthcare provider if NSAIDs are safe for you. Always read the medicine 
label and follow directions.
When should I seek immediate care? · You cannot move your joint at all. · You have severe pain that does not get better with medicine.
When should I contact my healthcare provider? · You have a fever. · You have redness or warmth over the joint. · The swelling does not decrease with treatment. · You have questions or concerns about your condition or care.
CARE AGREEMENT:
You have the right to help plan your care. Learn about your health condition and how it may be treated. Discuss 
treatment options with your caregivers to decide what care you want to receive. You always have the right to refuse 
treatment. The above information is an educational aid only. It is not intended as medical advice for individual 
conditions or treatments. Talk to your doctor, nurse or pharmacist before following any medical regimen to see if it is 
safe and effective for you.

Dear Mr. Kiper:
Thank you for visiting our office today. We understand sometimes you leave the office and 
then think of non-urgent questions you wish you had asked. Well, we’re here for you. Feel free

Your feedback is very important to us! You may receive a survey in the mail, and we hope you

Patient Summary as-of Visit (continued)
Clinical Notes
Subjective:
Patient ID: James R Kiper is a 41 yr/o male.
HPI
Sciatica: Occurred 7 years ago due to a herniated disc and it eventually resolved. MRI lumbar spine 2015 showed 
spondylosis L3-4. Last week, pain started back again without any obvious injury. Now pain is in the right side and 
radiates down the upper portion of the thigh. Since Wednesday, using tizanidine and ibuprofen, with some benefit. 
Tizanidine is causing drowsiness and headaches, however. 
The following portions of the patient's history were reviewed and updated as appropriate: allergies, current 
medications, past family history, past medical history, past social history, past surgical history and problem list.
Review of Systems 
Musculoskeletal: Positive for arthralgias and back pain. Negative for gait problem.

Injection Instructions
If you received an injection, a small reaction is not uncommon. A low grade fever (up to 100.5°F) and some pain is also
not uncommon during the first 24 hours, but unusual beyond. Call the office for a fever of 101.0°F or higher at any
time. Any expanding redness or persistent drainage from your injection site should be brought to the attention of your
doctor

December 20, 2017 
Dear Mr. Kiper:
Thank you for visiting our office today. We understand sometimes you leave the office and 
then think of non-urgent questions you wish you had asked. Well, we’re here for you. Feel free 
to use the attached questionnaire to ask those questions via MyNortonChart. A staff member 
or provider will be happy to answer any non-urgent questions you may have. 
Your feedback is very important to us! You may receive a survey in the mail, and we hope you 
will take the time to complete it. We value what you have to say and use your feedback to 
improve our patient care services here at Norton Healthcare. Thank you!
If this is a medical emergency, please call 911 or go to the nearest Norton Healthcare 
emergency department. 
We are privileged to be your choice for medical care. Wishing you good health.

Plaintiffs medical records are confidential pursuant to the following terms, restrictions and
conditions.
I . Information contained in JAMES RAY KIPER'S medical health care records and
pathology shall be disclosed or shared only with counsel of record in this action upon
written request or only to individuals certified by such counsel as employed by or assisting
counsel in preparation for, or at the trial of this action.
2. At the conclusion of the action, counsel for Defendant will return all copies of
medical or health care records, pathology and x-rays to the hospital with satisfactory
assurances that all copies have been destroyed.
3. Any such documents or information shall be used only for the purposes of this
litigation. The production ofsuch documents or information concerning Plaintiffs medical
and health care records shall not constitute a waiver of any privilege or other claim or right
of withholding or confidentially which Plaintiff may have.
4. A copy of the Order is as valid as the original.

Clinical Notes (continued)
Patient will be seen in the office in follow-up in 6 months.
During the of the visit the patient was educated and counseled about appropriate screening and preventive services. 
The following written plan was given to the patient.
1b. Get a dental checkup annually
2a. Exercise, aim for at least 20 minutes, 3 times a week
4a. Limit alcohol to less than 10 drinks a week. A drink is one beer, 4 ounces of wine, or 1.5 ounces of liquor.
5. Wear you safety belt
6i. Nutrition: look into ways to improve your diet, consider nutrition counseling.
6l. Medications: get help managing your medicine, consider using a weekly pillbox, or having someone help you 
arrange your pills for you.
6n. Sleep: improve your sleep habits, reduce sleeping medications
11b. Over the counter pain medications should be limited. They can cause stomach bleeding, and kidney or liver 
damage.
16b. Screening Test-Colonoscopy soul be preformed at least every 10 years until age 75 (or stool test more 
frequently) for Colorectal cancer. 
16f. Shots- get a Flu vaccine annually.
16j. Shots- get a Tetanus vaccine every 10 years.

Patient Instructions
Hypothyroidism
WHAT YOU NEED TO KNOW:
What is hypothyroidism? Hypothyroidism is a condition that develops when the thyroid gland does not make 
enough thyroid hormone. Thyroid hormones help control body temperature, heart rate, growth, and weight.

Patient Instructions (continued)
What causes hypothyroidism? If you have a family member with hypothyroidism, your risk is increased. Any of the 
following can cause hypothyroidism: · Autoimmune disease, such as inflammation of your thyroid, or Hashimoto disease
· Surgery, radiation therapy, or medicines such as lithium, sedatives, or narcotics
· Thyroid cancer or viral infection
· Low iodine levels
What are the signs and symptoms of hypothyroidism? The signs and symptoms may develop slowly, sometimes 
over several years. · Exhaustion
· Sensitivity to cold
· Headaches or decreased concentration
· Muscle aches or weakness

Patient Instructions (continued) · Constipation · Dry, flaky skin or brittle nails
· Thinning hair · Heavy or irregular monthly periods
· Depression or irritability
How is hypothyroidism diagnosed? Your healthcare provider will ask about your symptoms and what medicines 
you take. He will ask about your medical history and if anyone in your family has hypothyroidism. A blood test will 
show your thyroid hormone level. 
How is hypothyroidism treated? Thyroid hormone replacement medicine may bring your thyroid hormone level 
back to normal. Ask your healthcare provider for more information on other medicines you may need.
Call 911 for any of the following: · You have sudden chest pain or shortness of breath. · You have a seizure. · You feel like you are going to faint.
When should I seek immediate care? · You have diarrhea, tremors, or trouble sleeping. · Your legs, ankles, or feet are swollen.
When should I contact my healthcare provider? · You have a fever.

Hypothyroidism

Generated on 10/30/24 10:33 AM Page 648
What causes hypothyroidism? If you have a family member with hypothyroidism, your risk is increased. Any of the
following can cause hypothyroidism:
• Autoimmune disease, such as inflammation of your thyroid, or Hashimoto disease
• Surgery, radiation therapy, or medicines such as lithium, sedatives, or narcotics
• Thyroid cancer or viral infection
• Low iodine levels
What are the signs and symptoms of hypothyroidism? The signs and symptoms may develop slowly, sometimes
over several years.
• Exhaustion
Sensitivity to cold
Headaches or decreased concentration
Muscle aches or weakness
Constipation
Dry, flaky skin or brittle nails
Thinning hair
Heavy or irregular monthly periods
Depression or irritability
How is hypothyroidism diagnosed? Your healthcare provider will ask about your symptoms and what medicines you
take. He will ask about your medical history and if anyone in your family has hypothyroidism. A blood test will show
your thyroid hormone level.
How is hypothyroidism treated? Thyroid hormone replacement medicine may bring your thyroid hormone level back
to normal. Ask your healthcare provider for more information on other medicines you may need.

Results Callback
If you had labs or diagnostic tests ordered and you do not receive a phone call, letter or MyChart message regarding
your results within 2 weeks, please contact our office to receive your results.
Work Return/Restrictions
No documentation.
Injection Instructions
If you received an injection, a small reaction is not uncommon. A low grade fever (up to 100.5°F) and some pain is also
not uncommon during the first 24 hours, but unusual beyond. Call the office for a fever of 101.0°F or higher at any
time. Any expanding redness or persistent drainage from your injection site should be brought to the attention of your
doctor
After-Hours Instructions
For after-hours care, please call the office number and you will be routed to an on-call provider.
Norton eCare
Skip the waiting room for your non urgent health issue using Norton eCare through MyNortonChart.
Norton eCare providers are available 24 hours a day, seven days a week through two secure online options using your
MyNortonChart account: a video visit or an eVisit.
For additional information on conditions treated and how to schedule an eCare appointment, visit
NortonHealthcare.com/eCare.

April 22, 2019 
Dear Mr. Kiper:
Thank you for visiting our office today. We understand sometimes you leave the office and 
then think of non-urgent questions you wish you had asked. Well, we’re here for you. Feel free 
to use the attached questionnaire to ask those questions via MyNortonChart. A staff member 
or provider will be happy to answer any non-urgent questions you may have. 
Your feedback is very important to us! You may be receiving a survey through e-mail and we 
hope you will take the time to complete it. We value what you have to say and use your 
feedback to improve our patient care services here at Norton Healthcare. Thank you!
If this is a medical emergency, please call 911 or go to the nearest Norton Healthcare 
emergency department. 
We are privileged to be your choice for medical care. Wishing you good health.

Patient Summary as-of Visit (continued)
Clinical Notes
Pt returned call and states he is currently taking 75 mcg every am. Pt was informed that correct dose is 100 mcg and 
Pt states he will need a new rx for that dosage.
Left message for patient with results and recommendations. (Okay per PHI to LM). Informed patient to call the office if 
they have any questions. 
Need to verify if taking medication daily first thing in am with no other medications or eating 30 mins after taking 
synthroid. 
----- Message from Terrence P. Donohue, MD sent at 4/23/20194:39 PM EDT ----- Verify patient is taking his Synthroid 100 micrograms daily. Results demonstrate profound hypothyroidism with a TSH 
of 20.9 please increase Synthroid to 150 micrograms daily if indeed the patient is taking his medication. If he is not 
taking his medication please emphasize that he must take his thyroid replacement. Repeat in one month.

PATIENT:
James R. Kiper
DOB:
[DOB-7230]
Dear Dr. Fannin NMA,
I would liketo give you an update after our visit today.In an effort to keep unnecessary paperwork from
being generated, I am sending a short synopsis of my note. I would be happy to forward you a complete
note upon request.
ASSESSMENT/S:
eustachian tube dysfunction, headache, Chronic sinusitis, unspecified location, Postnasal drip,Perennial
allergic rhinitis, and lymphadenopathy
SUMMARY:
[DOB-4106]: This is a very pleasant 43 year old who we have not seen since 2017 and he reports
worsening allergic rhinitis symptoms despite the use of benadryl as needed. He reports a possible sinus
infection for 2 months. He has avoided Flonase as this caused fluid build up in his retina in the past with
steroid nasal spray. He had allergy testing in 2017 which showed severe allergies across-the-board and
he wanted to start immunotherapy though his insurance was an issue. On exam he has TM sclerosis
bilaterally, he has allergic nasal findings with no acute sinonasal inflammation and anteriorly or
posteriorly on endoscopy and his throat is clear. We discussed a good daily regimen of sinus rinses,
claritin, Singulair and Astelin and we will get him set up for repeat allergy testing and see him back after
this has been performed.
I appreciate the opportunity to be involved with the care of this patient. Please feel free to contact me
with any questions - cell [PHONE-4105].
initial
Wes A- Allison, M.D,
drwesallison(S)gmail.com
www.advancedentandallergy.com
cc:
enc:

Louisville,KY 40213

1725 Gagel Avenue
Louisville, KY 40216

Patient Instructions
Tendon Rupture
WHAT YOU NEED TO KNOW:
What is a tendon rupture? A tendon rupture is a partial or complete tear of your tendon. Tendons are tough bands of 
tissue that attach your muscles to your bones. A tear may be caused by an injury or increased pressure on the tendon 
that occurs during sports or a fall. Your risk may be higher if you have a weak tendon. Weak tendons may be caused 
by tendonitis, use of steroids, older age, and chronic conditions such as arthritis. 
What are the signs and symptoms of a tendon rupture? · Tearing or popping sound at the time of the injury
· Pain or tenderness in the area of the ruptured tendon
· Weakness or stiffness in the injured area
· Swelling · Bruising
· Trouble walking or moving the area where the tendon rupture occurred
How is a tendon rupture diagnosed? Your healthcare provider will ask about your symptoms and what you were 
doing at the time of your injury. He will also ask about any medical conditions you have and medicines you take. He 
will examine your tendon and check how well you can move the area in different directions. You may also need any of 
the following:
depressed or 
hopeless?
Little interest or 
pleasure in doing 
things?
No -TM at 06/26/19 1530
PHQ2 Score 0 -TM at 06/26/19 1530

[DOB-3192] - Office Visit in Norton Community Medical Associates - Audubon West 200 (continued)
Patient Instructions (continued) · X-rays may show if the tendon has completely separated from the bone. · An MRI takes pictures of your tendon to show the damage. You may be given liquid to help the pictures show 
up better. Tell a healthcare provider if you have ever had an allergic reaction to contrast liquid. Do not enter the 
MRI room with anything metal. Metal can cause serious injury. Tell a healthcare provider if you have any metal 
in or on your body. · An ultrasound uses sound waves to show pictures of your tendon on a monitor. An ultrasound may show a 
tear in the tendon.
How is a tendon rupture treated? Treatment depends on which tendon you ruptured and how severe the rupture is. 
You may need any of the following: · NSAIDs , such as ibuprofen, help decrease swelling, pain, and fever. This medicine is available with or without 
a doctor's order. NSAIDs can cause stomach bleeding or kidney problems in certain people. If you take blood 
thinner medicine, always ask your healthcare provider if NSAIDs are safe for you. Always read the medicine 
label and follow directions. · Acetaminophen decreases pain. It is available without a doctor's order. Ask how much to take and how often 
to take it. Follow directions. Acetaminophen can cause liver damage if not taken correctly. · A steroid injection decreases pain, inflammation, and helps heal a partial tear. · Support devices , such as a brace, cast, or splint, limit movement and protect your tendon. If the tendon 
rupture is in your leg, you may need to use crutches. This will decrease pain as you move around. · Physical therapy may be recommended after swelling and pain have decreased. A physical therapist teaches 
you exercises to help improve movement and strength. · Surgery may be needed to reattach your tendon to the bone if you have a complete tear.
How can I manage my symptoms? · Rest the injured tendon until pain and swelling have decreased. Ask your healthcare provider what activities 
you can do while your tendon heals.

[DOB-3193] - Office Visit in Norton Community Medical Associates - Audubon West 200 (continued)
Patient Instructions (continued) · Apply ice on your tendon for 15 to 20 minutes every hour for 48 hours or as directed. Use an ice pack, or put 
crushed ice in a plastic bag. Cover it with a towel. Ice helps prevent tissue damage and decreases swelling and 
pain. · Compress the injury with an elastic bandage, air cast, medical boot, or splint to reduce swelling. Ask your 
healthcare provider which compression device to use, and how tight it should be. · Elevate the injured area above the level of your heart as often as you can. This will help decrease swelling and 
pain. If possible, prop the injured area on pillows or blankets to keep it elevated comfortably.
When should I seek immediate care? · You have severe pain in the injured area, even after you take medicine. · Your arm or leg feels warm, tender, and painful. It may look swollen and red. · You feel lightheaded, short of breath, and have chest pain. · You cough up blood.
When should I contact my healthcare provider? · Your symptoms do not get better with treatment. · You feel another pop, snap, or crack in your tendon. · You have questions or concerns about your condition or care.
CARE AGREEMENT:
You have the right to help plan your care. Learn about your health condition and how it may be treated. Discuss 
treatment options with your healthcare providers to decide what care you want to receive. You always have the right to 
refuse treatment. The above information is an educational aid only. It is not intended as medical advice for individual 
conditions or treatments. Talk to your doctor, nurse or pharmacist before following any medical regimen to see if it is 
safe and effective for you.

Dear Mr. Kiper:
Thank you for visiting our office today. We understand sometimes you leave the office and 
then think of non-urgent questions you wish you had asked. Well, we’re here for you. Feel free 
to use the attached questionnaire to ask those questions via MyNortonChart. A staff member 
or provider will be happy to answer any non-urgent questions you may have. 
Your feedback is very important to us! You may be receiving a survey through e-mail and we 
hope you will take the time to complete it. We value what you have to say and use your 
feedback to improve our patient care services here at Norton Healthcare. Thank you!
If this is a medical emergency, please call 911 or go to the nearest Norton Healthcare 
emergency department. 
We are privileged to be your choice for medical care. Wishing you good health.

Clinical Notes (continued)
Subjective:
Subjective Patient ID: James R Kiper is a 43 yr/o male.
Patient is here today to be seen for a fall he sustained yesterday evening at 10 PM. He reports that he was going to 
sit back on a rolling chair at his home and slipped out of it. He reports that he landed on the right side of his coccyx, 
hit the right side of his lumbar/thoracic back against the wall, landed with his left hand/arm extended to catch himself 
on the ground, hit his left shoulder into the wall. He denies hitting his head. He reports that he is having symptoms 
consistent with whiplash. Reports that he feels worse than when he was in an MVA several years ago. Denies chest 
pain, shortness of breath, dizziness, headache, severe pain, decreased range of motion, memory issues, difficulty with 
speech, visual disturbance, etc. Vital signs stable
Fall
Using 800 milligrams ibuprofen p.r.n./ice
Reports these are not helping greatly with his symptoms
Reports persistent numbness/tingling on the right side of his buttocks
Consistent soreness left neck
Pain when he turns his neck/head
Soreness across right sided ribs
Soreness posterior left shoulder
Persistent soreness right thoracic/lumbar back
Denies decreased range of motion, severe pain
Patient reports he had a mild headache which has since resolved
Reports that he fell last year coming out of his shed
Reports that his symptoms healed within the first day or 2
He reports that his pain has remained consistent since injuring himself last evening
History of herniated disc greater than 10 years ago
Patient reports this has since resolved
History of right-sided sciatica greater than 10 years ago
MVA ×3 in 2015
Patient denies residual injury related to accidents
Thyroid enlargement
Patient reports history of goiter
Is unsure of when/what was done for treatment
Reports that he does not know the last time he had labs for his thyroid
The following portions of the patient's history were reviewed and updated as appropriate: allergies, current 
medications, past family history, past medical history, past social history, past surgical history and problem list.

We encourage you to follow these recommendations:
 1. Exercise regularly (3 hours per week minimum) and normalize your weight (BMI of less than 30).
2. See the eye doctor every two years after you turn 40 and the dentist every 6 months.
 3. Take your medications regularly as directed, and see your physician every 3-6 months ( or more often if 
instructed by doctor) for regular follow-up.
 4. We want your blood pressure to run below 140/90.
5. Avoid all tobacco use and any exposure to second hand smoke at home or work.
6. Please get a flu shot every fall, if not contraindicated.
7. Wear seatbelts whenever riding in a car or truck.
8. Make sure you keep up with age appropriate screenings and immunizations (Colonoscopy after age 50, PSA 
annually for men 50-75), mammograms annually for women 40-70, Pap smears every 2 years for women 21-65, 
Tetanus booster every 10 years, Pneumonia shot after age 50, shingles shot after age 50)
Fall Prevention
WHAT YOU NEED TO KNOW:
Fall prevention includes ways to make your home and other areas safer. It also includes ways you can move more 
carefully to prevent a fall. Health conditions that cause changes in your blood pressure, vision, or muscle strength and 
coordination may increase your risk for falls. Medicines may also increase your risk for falls if they make you dizzy, 
weak, or sleepy. 
DISCHARGE INSTRUCTIONS:
Call 911 or have someone else call if: · You have fallen and are unconscious. · You have fallen and cannot move part of your body.

Clinical Notes (continued)
Contact your healthcare provider if: · You have fallen and have pain or a headache. · You have questions or concerns about your condition or care.
Fall prevention tips: · Stand or sit up slowly. This may help you keep your balance and prevent falls. · Use assistive devices as directed. Your healthcare provider may suggest that you use a cane or walker to 
help you keep your balance. You may need to have grab bars put in your bathroom near the toilet or in the 
shower. · Wear shoes that fit well and have soles that grip. Wear shoes both inside and outside. Use slippers with 
good grip. Do not wear shoes with high heels. · Wear a personal alarm. This is a device that allows you to call 911 if you fall and need help. Ask your 
healthcare provider for more information. · Stay active. Exercise can help strengthen your muscles and improve your balance. Your healthcare provider 
may recommend water aerobics or walking. He or she may also recommend physical therapy to improve your 
coordination. Never start an exercise program without talking to your healthcare provider first. · Manage your medical conditions. Keep all appointments with your healthcare providers. Visit your eye doctor 
as directed.
Home safety tips: · Add items to prevent falls in the bathroom. Put nonslip strips on your bath or shower floor to prevent you 
from slipping. Use a bath mat if you do not have carpet in the bathroom. This will prevent you from falling when 
you step out of the bath or shower. Use a shower seat so you do not need to stand while you shower. Sit on the 
toilet or a chair in your bathroom to dry yourself and put on clothing. This will prevent you from losing your 
balance from drying or dressing yourself while you are standing.

Clinical Notes (continued) · Keep paths clear. Remove books, shoes, and other objects from walkways and stairs. Place cords for 
telephones and lamps out of the way so that you do not need to walk over them. Tape them down if you cannot 
move them. Remove small rugs. If you cannot remove a rug, secure it with double-sided tape. This will prevent 
you from tripping. · Install bright lights in your home. Use night lights to help light paths to the bathroom or kitchen. Always turn 
on the light before you start walking. · Keep items you use often on shelves within reach. Do not use a step stool to help you reach an item. · Paint or place reflective tape on the edges of your stairs. This will help you see the stairs better.
Follow up with your healthcare provider as directed: Write down your questions so you remember to ask them 
during your visits.

Steroid injection today.
Please take the flexeril at night before bed for 2 weeks for symptom relief. Do not take while operating machinery as

Please restart ibuprofen 24-48 hours after steroid injection as discussed.
Please take as directed.
Please let us know if you have any issues with these medications.
Follow-up with your PCP, Dr. Donahue regarding concerns for thyroid issues.
Thyroid ultrasound and labs ordered today.
Your prescription(s) were electronically sent to your pharmacy (or printed if they are for controlled substances).
Take all your medications as directed
We encourage you to follow these recommendations:
 1. Exercise regularly (3 hours per week minimum) and normalize your weight (BMI of less than 30).
2. See the eye doctor every two years after you turn 40 and the dentist every 6 months.
 3. Take your medications regularly as directed, and see your physician every 3-6 months ( or more often if 
instructed by doctor) for regular follow-up.
 4. We want your blood pressure to run below 140/90.
5. Avoid all tobacco use and any exposure to second hand smoke at home or work.
6. Please get a flu shot every fall, if not contraindicated.
7. Wear seatbelts whenever riding in a car or truck.
8. Make sure you keep up with age appropriate screenings and immunizations (Colonoscopy after age 50, PSA 
annually for men 50-75), mammograms annually for women 40-70, Pap smears every 2 years for women 21-65, 
Tetanus booster every 10 years, Pneumonia shot after age 50, shingles shot after age 50)
Fall Prevention
WHAT YOU NEED TO KNOW:
Fall prevention includes ways to make your home and other areas safer. It also includes ways you can move more 
carefully to prevent a fall. Health conditions that cause changes in your blood pressure, vision, or muscle strength and 
coordination may increase your risk for falls. Medicines may also increase your risk for falls if they make you dizzy, 
weak, or sleepy. 
DISCHARGE INSTRUCTIONS:
Call 911 or have someone else call if: · You have fallen and are unconscious. · You have fallen and cannot move part of your body.
Contact your healthcare provider if: · You have fallen and have pain or a headache. · You have questions or concerns about your condition or care.

Patient Instructions (continued)
Fall prevention tips: · Stand or sit up slowly. This may help you keep your balance and prevent falls. · Use assistive devices as directed. Your healthcare provider may suggest that you use a cane or walker to 
help you keep your balance. You may need to have grab bars put in your bathroom near the toilet or in the 
shower. · Wear shoes that fit well and have soles that grip. Wear shoes both inside and outside. Use slippers with 
good grip. Do not wear shoes with high heels. · Wear a personal alarm. This is a device that allows you to call 911 if you fall and need help. Ask your 
healthcare provider for more information. · Stay active. Exercise can help strengthen your muscles and improve your balance. Your healthcare provider 
may recommend water aerobics or walking. He or she may also recommend physical therapy to improve your 
coordination. Never start an exercise program without talking to your healthcare provider first. · Manage your medical conditions. Keep all appointments with your healthcare providers. Visit your eye doctor 
as directed.
Home safety tips: · Add items to prevent falls in the bathroom. Put nonslip strips on your bath or shower floor to prevent you 
from slipping. Use a bath mat if you do not have carpet in the bathroom. This will prevent you from falling when 
you step out of the bath or shower. Use a shower seat so you do not need to stand while you shower. Sit on the 
toilet or a chair in your bathroom to dry yourself and put on clothing. This will prevent you from losing your 
balance from drying or dressing yourself while you are standing. · Keep paths clear. Remove books, shoes, and other objects from walkways and stairs. Place cords for 
telephones and lamps out of the way so that you do not need to walk over them. Tape them down if you cannot 
move them. Remove small rugs. If you cannot remove a rug, secure it with double-sided tape. This will prevent 
you from tripping.

Fall Prevention
WHAT YOU NEED TO KNOW:
Fall prevention includes ways to make your home and other areas safer.It also includes ways you can move more
carefully to prevent a fall. Health conditions that cause changes in your blood pressure, vision, or muscle strength and
coordination may increase your risk for falls. Medicines may also increase your risk for falls if they make you dizzy, weak,
or sleepy.
DISCHARGE INSTRUCTIONS:
Call 911 or have someone else call if:
• You have fallen and are unconscious.
• You have fallen and cannot move part of your body.
Contact your healthcare provider if:
• You have fallen and have pain or a headache.
• You have questions or concerns about your condition or care.
Fall prevention tips:
• Stand or sit up slowly. This may help you keep your balance and prevent falls.

you keep your balance. You may need to have grab bars put in your bathroom near the toilet or in the shower.

Home safety tips:
• Add items to prevent falls in the bathroom. Put nonslip strips on your bath or shower floor to prevent you
from slipping. Use a bath mat if you do not have carpet in the bathroom. This will prevent you from falling when
you step out of the bath or shower. Use a shower seat so you do not need to stand while you shower. Sit on the
toilet or a chair in your bathroom to dry yourself and put on clothing. This will prevent you from losing your
balance from drying or dressing yourself while you are standing.
• Keep paths clear. Remove books, shoes, and other objects from walkways and stairs. Place cords for telephones
and lamps out of the way so that you do not need to walk over them. Tape them down if you cannot move them.
Remove small rugs.If you cannot remove a rug, secure it with double-sided tape. This will prevent you from
tripping.
• Install bright lights in your home. Use night lights to help light paths to the bathroom or kitchen. Always turn
on the light before you start walking.
• Keep items you use often on shelves within reach. Do not use a step stool to help you reach an item.
• Paint or place reflective tape on the edges of your stairs. This will help you see the stairs better.
Follow up with your healthcare provider as directed: Write down your questions so you remember to ask them
during your visits.

Results Callback
If you had labs or diagnostic tests ordered and you do not receive a phone call, letter or MyChart message regarding
your results within 2 weeks, please contact our office to receive your results.
Work Return/Restrictions
No documentation.
Injection Instructions
If you received an injection, a small reaction is not uncommon. A low grade fever (up to 100.5°F) and some pain is also
not uncommon during the first 24 hours, but unusual beyond. Call the office for a fever of 101.0°F or higher at any
time. Any expanding redness or persistent drainage from your injection site should be brought to the attention of your
doctor
After-Hours Instructions
For after-hours care, please call the office number and you will be routed to an on-call provider.
Norton eCare
Skip the waiting room for your non urgent health issue using Norton eCare through MyNortonChart.
Norton eCare providers are available 24 hours a day, seven days a week through two secure online options using your
MyNortonChart account: a video visit or an eVisit.
For additional information on conditions treated and how to schedule an eCare appointment, visit
NortonHealthcare.com/eCare.

September 11, 2019 
Dear Mr. Kiper:
Thank you for visiting our office today. We understand sometimes you leave the office and 
then think of non-urgent questions you wish you had asked. Well, we’re here for you. Feel free 
to use the attached questionnaire to ask those questions via MyNortonChart. A staff member 
or provider will be happy to answer any non-urgent questions you may have. 
Your feedback is very important to us! You may be receiving a survey through e-mail and we 
hope you will take the time to complete it. We value what you have to say and use your 
feedback to improve our patient care services here at Norton Healthcare. Thank you!
If this is a medical emergency, please call 911 or go to the nearest Norton Healthcare 
emergency department. 
We are privileged to be your choice for medical care. Wishing you good health.

Narrative:
RADIOLOGY REPORT
FACILITY: NORTON PHYSICIAN SERVICES
UNIT/AGE/GENDER: P.CMAAUDW OP AGE:43 Y SEX:M
PATIENT NAME/DOB: KIPER, JAMES, R [DOB-7230]
UNIT NUMBER: EP00479698
ACCOUNT NUMBER: 10074418341
ACCESSION NUMBER: EPPA19RAD755136
EPPA19RAD755137
EPPA19RAD755135
Date: September 11, 2019
EXAM: 5 view cervical spine, AP pelvis, bilateral sacroiliac, 5 views lumbar spine
HISTORY: Trauma, fall. Body aches.
FINDINGS:
5 views of the cervical spine: There is normal alignment of the cervical spine. There is no evidence for vertebral body 
compression fracture or subluxation. The facet alignment is maintained. The prevertebral soft tissues are normal. The 
neuroforamina 
appear patent. The odontoid view is normal.
Lumbar spine series: 5 views were obtained. There is mild disc height loss at L5-S1. There is normal alignment of the lumbar 
spine. There is no evidence of a compression fracture or subluxation injury. There is a moderate colonic fecal burden of the 
ascending colon. The transverse processes are intact.
AP pelvis, bilateral sacroiliac joints: The bony sacrum is intact. The sacroiliac joints are unremarkable.
IMPRESSION:
1. No evidence for acute fracture or malalignment of the cervical spine.
2. Mild degenerative disc height loss at L5-S1. No evidence of acute abnormality.
3. No evidence for displaced sacral fracture or sacroiliac joint disruption
Dictated by: Christopher Massey, M.D.
Images and Report reviewed and interpreted by: Christopher Massey, M.D.

Allergy
March 20, 2023
Terrence P. MD Donohue NMA
3101Poplar Level Road #101
Louisville, KY 40213
Fax: [PHONE-3629]
Dear Terrence P. MD Donohue NMA,
Thank you for allowing us to evaluate James Kiper on March 17, 2023.
March 17, 2023: James presents to the office today for an evaluation of his throat. For the past several days, he has had sinus
headache and a sore throat. He is concerned it might be allergy related. He has been trying allergy medications without relief. He has
not tested himself for COVID. He did have COVID last year. Examination today demonstrated absent tonsils with leftward septal
deviation. Fiberoptic endoscopy was performed today which demonstrated some minimal discolored mucus in the nasopharynx, but the
nasal airway was clear of purulence. His symptoms are likely viral. Plan to treat with a short course of Prednisone for 4 days. A
nasopharyngeal respiratory ID swab was performed today and he will be contacted with results. This likely represents a viral infection.
Thank you for allowing us to participate in James Kiper's care.
Regards,
Gregory Abbas, M.D.
Gregory Abbas,M.D.

1, Acute nasopharyngitis
Acute nasopharyngitis [common cold] (J00)
Plan: Nasal endoscopy.
Right Procedure: Nasal endoscopy,diagnostic
Left Procedure:Nasal endoscopy, diagnostic
Right Indication: Acute nasopharyngitis , Moderate to severe signs/symptoms of upper airway abnormalities in which a nasal speculum exam failed
to determine etiology: anosmia, hyposmia, hoarseness, epistaxis, facial pain,odynophagia, dysphagia, serosanguinous nasal discharge, tightness
in the throat, change in vocal quality, halitosis, apnea,or others, etc.
Left Indication:Acute nasopharyngitis , Moderate to severe signs/symptoms of upper airway abnormalities in which a nasal speculum exam failed
to determine etiology: anosmia, hyposmia, hoarseness, epistaxis, facial pain,odynophagia, dysphagia, serosanguinous nasal discharge, tightness
in the throat, change in vocal quality, halitosis, apnea,or others, etc.
Right Anesthesia: 4%topical tetracaine and topical oxymetazoline
Left Anesthesia: 4% topical tetracaine and topical oxymetazoline
Informed Consent:The benefits and risks of nasal endoscopy were discussed,including but not limited to:temporary pain or discomfort of the nose
or throat, temporary sensation of inability to swallow, temporary bad taste,remote possibility of fainting episode.
The flexible fiberoptic telescope was passed into the right naris and then passed atraumatically through the naris and through the posterior nasal
cavity to the nasopharynx, and used to examine the inferior, middle, and superior turbinates, the inferior,middle, and superior meati,and the
sphenoethmoid recess. Exam findings are detailed in the exam section.
The flexible fiberoptic telescope was passed into the left naris and then passed atraumatically through the naris and through the posterior nasal
cavity to the nasopharynx, and used to examine the inferior, middle, and superior turbinates, the inferior,middle, and superior meati,and the
sphenoethmoid recess. Exam findings are detailed in the exam section.
The patient tolerated the procedure well without complications.
Nasal exam findings:right intranasal endoscopic examination demonstrates a normal examination without turbinate hypertrophy, masses, septal
deformity or synechiae; the superior, inferior, and middle meati are clear; no masses or ulcers;the sphenoethmoidal recess is clear and normal; no
drainage; and left intranasal endoscopic examination demonstrates a normal examination without turbinate hypertrophy, masses, septal deformity
or synechiae; the superior, inferior, and middle meati are clear; no masses or ulcers;the sphenoethmoidal recess is clear and normal; no drainage;
Additional Nasopharyngeal exam findings: There was some minimal discolored mucus
2. Acute nasopharyngitis
Acute nasopharyngitis [common cold] (J00)
Plan: Counseling - acute nasopharyngitis.
I counseled the patient regarding the following:
Management: Rest, fluids, and pain relievers are all helpful in recovering from and upper respiratory infection.Patients with the common cold may
consider returning to their usual physical activity, including aerobic activity, if their symptoms are limited to the nose and throat.
Expectations: Viral infections account for the majority of upper respiratory infections. Antibiotics can help treat sinus and throat infections caused
by bacteria but will not help treat infections caused by viruses. Common symptoms of viral infections are sore throat,body aches, nasal

Note:
March 17, 2023: James presents to the office today for an evaluation of his throat. For the past several days, he has had sinus headache and a sore
throat. He is concerned it might be allergy related. He has been trying allergy medications without relief. He has not tested himself for COVID. He did
have COVID last year. Examination today demonstrated absent tonsils with leftward septal deviation. Fiberoptic endoscopy was performed today which
demonstrated some minimal discolored mucus in the nasopharynx,but the nasal airway was clear of purulence. His symptoms are likely viral. Plan to
treat with a short course of Prednisone for 4 days. A nasopharyngeal respiratory ID swab was performed today and he will be contacted with results.
This likely represents a viral infection.

Dear Dr. Fannin NMA,
I would liketo give you an update after our visit today.In an effort to keep unnecessary paperwork from
being generated, I am sending a short synopsis of my note. I would be happy to forward you a complete
note upon request.
ASSESSMENT/S:
eustachian tube dysfunction, headache, Chronic sinusitis, unspecified location, Postnasal drip,Perennial
allergic rhinitis, thyroid nodule, and Hypothyroid
SUMMARY:
10-8-19 This is a very pleasant 43 year old who has done well in regard to his allergies and he is not
requiring frequent daily sprays or antihistamines. Previously he had significant allergy testing this past
spring but chose to hold off on immunotherapy. He does have a past history of hypothyroidism and is
currently on Synthroid 100 meg for over 12 years. A recent ultrasound was performed given a slight
globus sensation and this showed a 4 mm right lower thyroid nodule with a heterogeneous gland
appearance consistent with his thyroiditis. He has no family history of thyroid cancer and no personal
history of radiation. On exam he has minimal allergic nasal findings, he has no palpable thyroid
abnormalities. We discussed that an FNA is not currently indicated and we will plan on a further thyroid
ultrasound in one year at a Norton facility for continuity and I can see him after this is completed.
I appreciate the opportunity to be involved with the care of this patient. Please feel free to contact me
with any questions - cell [PHONE-3647].

[DOB-3758] - Office Visit in Norton Community Medical Associates - Audubon West 200 (continued)
Clinical Notes (continued)
James R Kiper is a 43 yr/o male who presents to the office today for follow-up of hypothyroidism. The patient in the 
past was found to have Hashimoto's thyroiditis and has been placed on thyroid replacement therapy. He takes 
Synthroid 100 micrograms daily.
Ultrasound of the thyroid performed because of the large thyroid had demonstrated a 4 millimeter right lower pole 
nodule slightly hypervascular gland can be seen with thyroiditis.
ROS
Review of Systems 
Constitutional: Negative for chills and fever. 
HENT: Negative. 
Eyes: Negative for pain and discharge. 
Respiratory: Negative for cough and shortness of breath. 
Cardiovascular: Negative for chest pain and palpitations. 
Gastrointestinal: Negative for abdominal pain and nausea. 
Musculoskeletal: Negative. 
Skin: Negative for rash. 
 Negative for itching
Neurological: Negative. 
Psychiatric/Behavioral: Negative.

PHYSICAL EXAM
Vital Signs: BP 112/70 | Pulse 80 | Ht 5' 8" (1.727 m) | Wt 148 lb (67.1 kg) | SpO2 99% | BMI 22.50 kg/m² 
Constitutional: Well-developed well-nourished 43-year-old gentleman currently in no acute distress
HENT: Normocephalic, Atraumatic, Bilateral external ears normal, Oropharynx moist, No oral exudates, Nose normal. 
Neck- Normal range of motion, No tenderness, Supple, No stridor. Very subtle thyroid nodule palpated
Eyes: PERRL, EOMI, Conjunctiva normal, No discharge. 
Respiratory: Normal breath sounds, No respiratory distress, No wheezing, No chest tenderness. 
Cardiovascular: Normal heart rate, Normal rhythm, No murmurs, No rubs, No gallops. 
GI: Bowel sounds normal, Soft, No tenderness, No masses, No pulsatile masses. 
GU: No CVA tenderness.
Musculoskeletal: Intact distal pulses, No edema, No tenderness, No cyanosis, No clubbing. Good range of motion in 
all major joints. No tenderness to palpation or major deformities noted. Back- No tenderness. 
Integument: Warm, Dry, No erythema, No rash. 
Lymphatic: No lymphadenopathy noted. 
Neurologic: Alert & oriented x 3, Normal motor function, Normal sensory function, No focal deficits noted. 
Psychiatric: Affect normal, Judgment normal, Mood normal. 
ASSESSMENT AND PLAN 
James was seen today for hypothyroidism.
Diagnoses and all orders for this visit:
Hypothyroidism, unspecified type

Thyroid nodule
Patient has a strong family history of hypothyroidism. Grandmother had a hypothyroidism requiring thyroid 
replacement therapy mother had Graves' disease and sister has thyroid disease as well. Patient will be seen in the 
office in follow-up in 6 months repeat thyroid ultrasound will be performed at that time. Currently thyroid nodule is so 
small no action to be taken

Patient Instructions
Hypothyroidism
WHAT YOU NEED TO KNOW:
What is hypothyroidism? Hypothyroidism is a condition that develops when the thyroid gland does not make 
enough thyroid hormone. Thyroid hormones help control body temperature, heart rate, growth, and weight.
What causes hypothyroidism? If you have a family member with hypothyroidism, your risk is increased. Any of the 
following can cause hypothyroidism: · Autoimmune disease, such as inflammation of your thyroid, or Hashimoto disease
· Surgery, radiation therapy, or medicines such as lithium, sedatives, or narcotics
· Thyroid cancer or viral infection
· Low iodine levels
What are the signs and symptoms of hypothyroidism? The signs and symptoms may develop slowly, sometimes 
Initials Name Provider Type Discipline

[DOB-3840] - Office Visit in Norton Community Medical Associates - Audubon West 200 (continued)
Patient Instructions (continued)
over several years. · Exhaustion
· Sensitivity to cold
· Headaches or decreased concentration
· Muscle aches or weakness
· Constipation · Dry, flaky skin or brittle nails
· Thinning hair · Heavy or irregular monthly periods
· Depression or irritability
How is hypothyroidism diagnosed? Your healthcare provider will ask about your symptoms and what medicines 
you take. He will ask about your medical history and if anyone in your family has hypothyroidism. A blood test will 
show your thyroid hormone level. 
How is hypothyroidism treated? Thyroid hormone replacement medicine may bring your thyroid hormone level 
back to normal. Ask your healthcare provider for more information on other medicines you may need.
Call 911 for any of the following: · You have sudden chest pain or shortness of breath. · You have a seizure. · You feel like you are going to faint.

Patient Instructions (continued)
When should I seek immediate care? · You have diarrhea, tremors, or trouble sleeping. · Your legs, ankles, or feet are swollen.
When should I contact my healthcare provider? · You have a fever. · You have chills, a cough, or feel weak and achy. · You have pain and swelling in your muscles and joints. · Your skin is itchy, swollen, or you have a rash. · Your signs and symptoms return or get worse, even after treatment. · You have questions or concerns about your condition or care.
CARE AGREEMENT:
You have the right to help plan your care. Learn about your health condition and how it may be treated. Discuss 
treatment options with your healthcare providers to decide what care you want to receive. You always have the right to 
refuse treatment. The above information is an educational aid only. It is not intended as medical advice for individual 
conditions or treatments. Talk to your doctor, nurse or pharmacist before following any medical regimen to see if it is 
safe and effective for you.

October 22, 2019 
Dear Mr. Kiper:
Thank you for visiting our office today. We understand sometimes you leave the office and 
then think of non-urgent questions you wish you had asked. Well, we’re here for you. Feel free 
to use the attached questionnaire to ask those questions via MyNortonChart. A staff member 
or provider will be happy to answer any non-urgent questions you may have. 
Your feedback is very important to us! You may be receiving a survey through e-mail and we 
hope you will take the time to complete it. We value what you have to say and use your 
feedback to improve our patient care services here at Norton Healthcare. Thank you!
If this is a medical emergency, please call 911 or go to the nearest Norton Healthcare 
emergency department. 
We are privileged to be your choice for medical care. Wishing you good health.

Physical Exam
Constitutional: He is oriented to person, place, and time. He appears well-developed and well-nourished. No distress. 
HENT: 
Head: Normocephalic and atraumatic. 
Mouth/Throat: Oropharyngeal exudate (hyperemic throat) present. 
Eyes: Pupils are equal, round, and reactive to light. Conjunctivae are normal. 
Neck: Normal range of motion. Neck supple. 
Cardiovascular: Normal rate and regular rhythm. 
Pulmonary/Chest: Effort normal and breath sounds normal. 
Musculoskeletal: 
 General: No tenderness or edema. 
Neurological: He is alert and oriented to person, place, and time. 
Skin: He is not diaphoretic. 
Psychiatric: His behavior is normal. 
Nursing note and vitals reviewed.
Assessment and Plan
James was seen today for office visit and sore throat.
Diagnoses and all orders for this visit:
Acute pharyngitis, unspecified etiology
-azithromycin (ZITHROMAX Z-PAK) 250 MG tablet; Take 2 tablets by mouth once for 1 dose 2 tabs today and
1 tab daily for total of 5 days. -Chlorcyclizine-Pseudoephed 25-60 MG TABS; Take 0.5 tablets by mouth 3 (three) times daily for 7 days. -naproxen (NAPROSYN) 500 MG tablet; Take 1 tablet by mouth 2 (two) times daily with meals.
 Advised to also do salt water gargles.
Return if symptoms worsen or fail to improve.

Results Callback
If you had labs or diagnostic tests ordered and you do not receive a phone call, letter or MyChart message regarding
your results within 2 weeks, please contact our office to receive your results.
Work Return/Restrictions
No documentation.
Injection Instructions
If you received an injection, a small reaction is not uncommon. A low grade fever (up to 100.5°F) and some pain is also
not uncommon during the first 24 hours, but unusual beyond. Call the office for a fever of 101.0°F or higher at any
time. Any expanding redness or persistent drainage from your injection site should be brought to the attention of your
doctor
